name: Young ass avoids food eaten by animals before being slaughtered. Runs to his mother and asks to have all remains of the hog's food taken out.

name: Wager : raja's daughter will bring servant dinner in field . Merchant ignorant that she is his wife.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Tiger, jackal, and bear each learn to fear man. They report him as head-thumper (he has beaten the tiger), turn-twister (he has swung jackal by tail), and top-tumbler (he has fallen out of tree on bear).

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

additional_description: They report him as head-thumper (he has beaten the tiger), turn-twister (he has swung jackal by tail), and top-tumbler (he has fallen out of tree on bear).

name: Wisdom acquired from beating. Incognito prince, beaten for his courtesy, realizes his folly and returns home.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
additional_description: Incognito prince, beaten for his courtesy, realizes his folly and returns home.

name: Counsels proved wise by experience.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: "Consider the end": counsel proved wise by experience. Barber hired to cut king's throat sees on the bottom of the basin the words "Whatever you do, do wisely and think of the consequences." He drops the razor and confesses.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
bibliographies: *Type 910C; *Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 20 No. 69; Alphabet No. 156; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Jewish: bin Gorion Born Judas IV 172, 283; India: Thompson-Balys: BENGAL . Bradley - Birt 59-61 No. 11 ; McCulloch 33. - BOMBAY . Guzarat : Kincaid 84 .

name: "Do not act when angry": counsel proved wise by experience. Man returns home and sees someone sleeping with his wife. Though he thinks it is a paramour, he restrains himself and finds that it is a newborn son.

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
bibliographies: *Type 910B; Chauvin II 157; Irish myth: Cross; Icelandic: Boberg; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: HIMALAYA . Haughton 193. - CENT . INDIA . Taylor 83. - CEYLON . Parker III 232f . Cf. Wienert FFC LVI 37; Phaedrus III No. 10.
additional_description: Man returns home and sees someone sleeping with his wife. Though he thinks it is a paramour, he restrains himself and finds that it is a newborn son.

name: "Never attack anyone before unsheathing and returning sword three times": counsel proved wise by experience.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: Sleep before committing suicide. After sleeping you will feel differently about suicide.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
additional_description: After sleeping you will feel differently about suicide.

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: "Take side road rather than main one where three roads meet": counsel proved wise by experience.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: "A way short yet long": counsel proved wise by experience. (Cf. J266.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: "Do not cross a bridge without dismounting from your horse"; counsel proved wise by experience. Man breaks leg.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
additional_description: Man breaks leg.

name: "Never wager more than a groat": counsel proved wise by experience. Man loses wife on wager.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: "Do not bet": counsel proved wise by experience.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: "Do not visit your friends often": counsel proved wise by experience. At last the man is treated shamefully.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
additional_description: At last the man is treated shamefully.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: "Do not lend out your horse": counsel proved wise by experience.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: "Do not walk half a mile with a man without asking his name": counsel proved wise by experience. Man runs race unwittingly with his wife's paramour and loses his wife on the wager. (Cf. J21.8.)

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
additional_description: Man runs race unwittingly with his wife's paramour and loses his wife on the wager.

name: "Rue not a thing that is past": counsel proved wise by experience. Man lets bird go and then, having listened to bird's false declaration that she had a precious gem in her body, he tries to climb a tree after her and falls.

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
bibliographies: *BP IV 149 n. 2; Gaster Exempla 256 No. 390; *Paris Légendes du moyen âge 225ff.; *Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 24; Alphabet No. 191; Oesterley Gesta Romanorum No. 167.
additional_description: Man lets bird go and then, having listened to bird's false declaration that she had a precious gem in her body, he tries to climb a tree after her and falls.

name: "Never believe what is beyond belief": counsel proved wise by experience. Man believes when bird tells him that she has a precious gem in her body. (Cf. J21.12, K604.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).
bibliographies: *BP IV 149 n. 2; Alphabet No. 191; Gaster Exempla 256 No. 390; Paris Légendes du moyen âge 225ff.; Oesterley Gesta Romanorum No. 167; Spanish Exempla: Keller.

name: "Never try to reach the unattainable": counsel proved wise by experience. (Cf. K604.)

division1: J0–J199. Acquisition and possession of wisdom (knowledge).

name: Wisdom from the Hebrews.

name: Choices.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Hobson's choice: choose what is put before you or nothing.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice between eggs: one egg or none.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice between evils.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Four choices, all of which are evil; man to make one choice only.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice: free poverty or enslaved wealth.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Ass brays on hearing a conch shell . Owner thinks he must have been a saint in a former life.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: U.P. Oudh : Crooke - Rouse 207 No. 21

name: Philosopher chooses poverty with freedom.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man gives all his wealth away, for it is better to die than to give up virtue.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Temple Legends III 69 .

name: Town mouse and country mouse. Latter prefers poverty with safety.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Type 112; Crane Vitry 199 No. 157; Wienert FFC LVI *59 (ET 208), 124 (ST 325); Halm Aesop No. 297; Jacobs Aesop 202 No. 7; Albini Atene e Roma VI 175. Spanish Exempla: Keller; Chinese: Eberhard FFC CXX 15 No. 5; Japanese: Ikeda.
additional_description: Latter prefers poverty with safety.

name: Fly jeers at king's elephant for his lack of freedom.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice: plainness with safety or grandeur with danger.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Ass envies horse in fine trappings. Horse killed in battle; ass content.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Jacobs Aesop 220 No. 78; Scala Celi 135a No. 744; India: Thompson-Balys: KASHMIR . Knowles Proverbs 86
additional_description: Horse killed in battle; ass content.

name: Choice: loss of beauty or speech. Latter chosen.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Latter chosen.

name: Choice: suffering in youth or old age.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Märchen 236; Fb "rig" III 55a; Irish myth: Cross; English: Wells 114 (Sir Isumbras); Lithuanian: Balys Index No. *937; Russian: Andrejev No. *931 I; Rumanian: Schullerus FFC LXXVIII No. 948*.

name: Present evil preferred to change for worse.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Don't drive away the flies. Wounded animal (man) refuses to have the flies driven away since they are now sated and their places will be taken by fierce and hungry flies.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 186; Wienert FFC LVI 60 (ET 228), 115 (ST 255); Halm Aesop No. 36; Jacobs Aesop 215 No. 64; Scala Celi 21a No. 132; Alphabet No. 97; Oesterley Gesta Romanorum No. 51; Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 32; Spanish Exempla: Keller.
additional_description: Wounded animal (man) refuses to have the flies driven away since they are now sated and their places will be taken by fierce and hungry flies.

name: Don't set a hungry guard over food. Parrot set to guard figs eats his fill. When replaced he calls attention to the fact that he is now full and therefore safer than another hungry parrot.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Parrot set to guard figs eats his fill. When replaced he calls attention to the fact that he is now full and therefore safer than another hungry parrot.

name: King refuses to exile gossipers. They would defame him among strangers. At home they serve to test the king's patience and to reform his life.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: They would defame him among strangers. At home they serve to test the king's patience and to reform his life.

name: Do not pluck off the well-fed leeches. Wolf told not to do so lest hungrier ones take their places as he swims.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Wolf told not to do so lest hungrier ones take their places as he swims.

name: Old man chooses to be annoyed by occasional and loud chirp of swallows than by never-ending but soft chirp of sandpipers.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Oxen decide not to kill butchers, since inexpert killers might replace them.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Old woman prays for safety of cruel tyrant for fear a worse one will succeed him.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Heathcock prefers home with hardships to travel in foreign lands.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Monk goes to wilderness to escape work on material things. Finds that he must work to live and returns to monastery.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Finds that he must work to live and returns to monastery.

name: Choice of deaths.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Army faces enemy rather than the anger of their king who would kill them if they returned in flight.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Lamb prefers to be sacrificed in temple rather than to be eaten by a wolf.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 51 (ET 107), 115 (ST 253); Halm Aesop No. 273.

name: Crab would rather be killed outright than imprisoned and starved.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Santal : Bompas 31lf . No. 106 .

name: Soldier asks to be stabbed in chest. Prostrate soldier asks enemy to stab him in chest instead of back in order to save his honor. Captor frees him and the two become friends.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Prostrate soldier asks enemy to stab him in chest instead of back in order to save his honor. Captor frees him and the two become friends.

name: Early death with fame preferred.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Saint chooses to die "after pride of youth" and before "misery of old age."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Captivity preferred to death.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Unsatisfactory life preferred to death.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Trickster overhears man praying for death to take him; the trickster appears at man's house, usually in disguise, says he is God (or the devil). The man tells him to take his wife (or he runs away). (Compare C11 for a similar situation in which Death appears.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The man tells him to take his wife (or he runs away).

name: Escaped lamb delivers himself to shepherd rather than to slaughter.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 71 (ET 354), 115 (ST 252); Halm Aesop No. 377.

name: Discontented ass longs for death but changes mind when he sees skins of dead asses at a fair.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Enemies make peace rather than slay each other.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Lion and wild boar make peace rather than slay each other for benefit of vulture.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 48 (ET 72), 108 (ST 202); Halm Aesop No. 253.

name: Choice: small injustice permitted rather than to cause troubles of state.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: King overlooks wife's unfaithfulness rather than to cause troubles of state.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Senator overlooks wife's adultery rather than impair his reputation.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man rebukes servants for telling him of his wife's unfaithfulness.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: King chooses small inconvenience of personal troubles to great troubles for his kingdom. He suffers to help realm.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: He suffers to help realm.

name: Man would rather pay 500 florins he did not owe than have it said he did not pay debts.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Rescue alone from shipwreck chosen over drowning with goods.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice between evils: pay tribute or lose both money and life.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice: apparent injustice over greater wrong.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Angel and hermit. Angel takes hermit with him and does many seemingly unjust things. Later shows why each of these was just.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Type 759; *BP IV 326 No. 3; **DeCock Studien en Essays 178ff.; *Crane Vitry 179 No. 109; Herbert III 8; Fb "uskyldig"; Alphabet Nos. 68, 411; Scala Celi 15a No. 85; Hdwb. d. Märchens I 508b nn. 9–11; *Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 32; **O. Rohde Die Erzählung vom Einsiedler und dem Engel (Leipzig, 1894); *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 682. – Irish: Beal XXI 336; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Jewish: bin Gorion Born Judas III 23, *296, Goebel Jüdische Motive in märchenhaften Erzählungsgut (Gleiwitz, 1932) 116ff., *Neuman.
additional_description: Angel takes hermit with him and does many seemingly unjust things. Later shows why each of these was just.

name: Angel explains to hermit why God lets a sinner die in peace and have big funeral while holy hermit is slain by a wild beast.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: God punishes many men because of one sinner, like a man who kills hive of bees for stinging of one.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Angel in form of young man shows skeptical hermit that ways of providence are inscrutable.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Youth made lame: had kicked his mother.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Lion sent to kill a man: frees him from possibility of sinning and sojourn in purgatory.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Angel takes cup from old man. Done lest he love the cup too much.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Done lest he love the cup too much.

name: Angel (Jesus) kills man. Done because man is plotting a murder.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Type 759; Wesselski Mönchslatein No. 79; Spanish Exempla: Keller.
additional_description: Done because man is plotting a murder.

name: Angel kills man because he loves his child too much.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Saint gives liberally to gambler, little to beggar. Gambler is generous, beggar hoards.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Gambler is generous, beggar hoards.

name: Forestman who longs to do evil is sent to hell: writer, who repents, is sent to heaven. God justifies this to his sage.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MADRAS . Telugu : Pantulu Ind . Antiq . XXVI 55 No. 12 .
additional_description: God justifies this to his sage.

name: Evil mother has fine funeral, good father poor.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Difficult choice between relatives.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice of freeing one son: adopted son or long-missing son.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice: blind son with long life or healthy son with short. Latter chosen.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MYSORE . Kincaid Nursery Tales 19 .
additional_description: Latter chosen.

name: Choice: foolish son always with him or four wise daughters who will leave him. Latter chosen.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MADRAS . Natesa Sastri Folklore 140 .
additional_description: Latter chosen.

name: Death preferred to other evils.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Death preferred to captivity.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Death preferred to dishonor.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice between evils – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Choice: staying at home with loving wife or going to tavern and having unfaithful wife. Man chooses latter.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man chooses latter.

name: Sheep and ignorant shearer. Had rather die than suffer longer from him.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 72 (ET 366), 115 (ST 251); Halm Aesop No. 382.
additional_description: Had rather die than suffer longer from him.

name: Choice: a big piece of cake with my curse or a small piece with my blessing.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Better send an ugly woman to the devil than a pretty one. Man chooses ugly mistress.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man chooses ugly mistress.

name: Choice between bad master, bad official, or bad neighbor. Bad master can do evil if he desires to do so; bad official can harm a poor person and complain against him to his master; bad neighbor can betray secret things about his neighbors. Bad neighbor worst.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Bad master can do evil if he desires to do so; bad official can harm a poor person and complain against him to his master; bad neighbor can betray secret things about his neighbors. Bad neighbor worst.

name: Bad choice between poor and miserly man. Neither makes good leader.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Neither makes good leader.

name: Rower prefers to be stoned by his master rather than remain out in the storm.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Contentment with evil master for fear of worse successor.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Scala Celi 21a No. 128; Herbert III 35ff.; Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 45; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.

name: Weaver prefers master with one hedgehog. Insists on his master putting hedgehog out of house. When master refuses, weaver leaves. Next master has two hedgehogs, and next has three. Weaver returns to first master.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Insists on his master putting hedgehog out of house. When master refuses, weaver leaves. Next master has two hedgehogs, and next has three. Weaver returns to first master.

name: Man retains questionable bride for fear of getting one who is worse.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The smaller the evil the better. Therefore choose the smallest woman possible for a bride.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Therefore choose the smallest woman possible for a bride.

name: Take money instead of revenge. Fool advises uncle, in letter which he did not send, to take money from wife's paramour instead of revenge.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Fool advises uncle, in letter which he did not send, to take money from wife's paramour instead of revenge.

name: Prisoners given choice between emasculation and blinding.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: God's punishment: the sinner may have twelve years of famine or twelve hours of heavy rainfall.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Physical pain preferred to poverty.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

section: J230. Choice: real and apparent values.

division3: J230–J299. Real and apparent values.
section: J230. Choice: real and apparent values.

When the master returns and tells the fool to tell the truth, the latter replies, "There is nothing worse on earth than the truth."

section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).

name: Dervish who stops work. Sees bird feed its young and decides that God takes care of everyone without work. He is shown his mistake.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).

name: King (queen) teaches children to work at all tasks to prepare them for life's possible hazards.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).

name: Planning for the greater office.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).

name: Looking for the keys of the abbey. Monk goes about with downcast eyes until he is made abbot; then lives in luxury. He explains that he was looking for the keys of the abbey. Now he has them.

division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).
additional_description: Monk goes about with downcast eyes until he is made abbot; then lives in luxury. He explains that he was looking for the keys of the abbey. Now he has them.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).
additional_description: Bishop refuses to eat small fish as he used to when an abbot. "Then I used small fish to catch big ones I have now!"

name: Safe provision for life not to be lightly surrendered.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).

name: Priest must give up his charge or his mistress. Gives up his parish and immediately loses his fickle mistress.

division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).

name: Acquisition of wealth.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).

name: Be diligent and spend little: how to become wealthy. Advice of a self-made man.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J700. Forethought in provision for life (general).
additional_description: Advice of a self-made man.

name: Wealth is most important.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Forethought in provision for food.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J710. Forethought in provision for food.

division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J710. Forethought in provision for food.

section: J710. Forethought in provision for food.

division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J710. Forethought in provision for food.

division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J710. Forethought in provision for food.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J710. Forethought in provision for food.

section: J730. Forethought in provision for clothing.

section: J730. Forethought in provision for clothing.

name: Forethought in provision for shelter.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J740. Forethought in provision for shelter.

name: Bear builds house of wood; fox of ice. Fox's house fails him in summer.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J700–J749. Forethought in provision for live.
section: J740. Forethought in provision for shelter.
additional_description: Fox's house fails him in summer.

name: Consider difficulties of course you are about to undertake.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Truth the best policy. Servant about to be caught for theft rehearses the lie he is to tell his master. He finds lies so transparent that he decides to tell the truth.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
additional_description: Servant about to be caught for theft rehearses the lie he is to tell his master. He finds lies so transparent that he decides to tell the truth.

name: Lady answers queen so straightforwardly she gets light punishment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: In planning future, profit by the past.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Frogs decide not to jump into the well. Their spring having dried up, they consider jumping into a well. They decide that the well may also dry up.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 59 (ET 202), 108 (ST 201); Halm Aesop No. 74; Italian Novella: Rotunda (J742).
additional_description: Their spring having dried up, they consider jumping into a well. They decide that the well may also dry up.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: The only person in the bath. Servant reports to master that there is but one person in the public bath Master finds three hundred. Only one person had removed stone from his path; rest had stubbed toes. He was only one worthy of the name of man.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
additional_description: Servant reports to master that there is but one person in the public bath Master finds three hundred. Only one person had removed stone from his path; rest had stubbed toes. He was only one worthy of the name of man.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
additional_description: Man allowed to manage the weather forgets to ask help of the wind. All goes wrong and he must give up management.

name: Advice after the event valueless.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Doctor gives advice after patient dies. Scorned for lack of foresight.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI *84 (ET 509), 108 (ST 204); Halm Aesop No. 169.
additional_description: Scorned for lack of foresight.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Tailless fox tries in vain to induce foxes to cut off tails.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Tailless jackal persuades other jackals to cut off tails.

division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Fish refuse fox's invitation to live on dry land and thus escape danger of fishermen.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Old age must be planned for.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
additional_description: Father sees that it is time for him to reform.

name: Fourth horse must carry all. Miller has four horses to carry grain. He uses only one, so that it soon dies. Four horses are childhood, youth, manhood, and old age. Don't heap all burden of securing salvation on the fourth horse, old age.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
additional_description: Miller has four horses to carry grain. He uses only one, so that it soon dies. Four horses are childhood, youth, manhood, and old age. Don't heap all burden of securing salvation on the fourth horse, old age.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
additional_description: Charges a pair of shoes to consort with men. When old she pays with shoes the men who will consort with her.

name: Leave a loophole for escape.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Priest keeps in container relic which when kissed renders people immune from pestilence. They only kiss container, so that if they die he will not be held responsible.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.
additional_description: They only kiss container, so that if they die he will not be held responsible.

name: Mark the way one is going in an unfamiliar country.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Cat only drives rats away ; if she killed them she would no longer have employ- ment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Do not work yourself out of employment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Cat only drives rats away; if she killed them she would no longer have employment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J750–J799. Forethought – miscellaneous.

name: Policy in dealing with the great.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Wisdom of concessions to power.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: The lion's share. Ass divides booty equally between himself, fox, and lion. Lion eats ass. Fox then divides: gives lion meat and he takes bones.

section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

additional_description: Ass divides booty equally between himself, fox, and lion. Lion eats ass. Fox then divides: gives lion meat and he takes bones.

name: Lion divides the booty. Best part goes to himself as king of beasts; second, as strongest; third, as most valiant; fourth – "touch it if you dare."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI *59 (ET 216), *148 (ST 530); Halm Aesop No 258; *K. Gorski Die Fabel vom Löwenanteil in ihrer geschichtlichen Entwicklung (Berlin, 1888).
additional_description: Best part goes to himself as king of beasts; second, as strongest; third, as most valiant; fourth "touch it if you dare."

name: Fox refuses to mediate between lion and lioness. Lion decides to abandon lioness because of her bad odor. Ass, hog, and fox as judges. Ass says she has bad odor: lioness slaps him. Hog says she has not: lion slaps him. Fox says that he has a bad cold and cannot smell.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: Lion decides to abandon lioness because of her bad odor. Ass, hog, and fox as judges. Ass says she has bad odor: lioness slaps him. Hog says she has not: lion slaps him. Fox says that he has a bad cold and cannot smell.

name: King honors poet and critic: the first so that he will honor the king; the second, so that he will not dishonor him.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Ruler angered by evil spoken of him is placated by soft words of speakers.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Prime minister bribes priest to persuade king that ocean of milk he wishes sought for had curdled.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Fox with three hundred fables ready to tell against lion conveniently forgets them.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Flattery of the great.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Imprisoned musician defends himself. Has been imprisoned because the king did not like the way the musician looked at him. After a year the king returns as a conqueror and sees musician. The latter says that he saw the king's conquests in a vision and was blessing him when he looked at him. The musician is honored.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: Has been imprisoned because the king did not like the way the musician looked at him. After a year the king returns as a conqueror and sees musician. The latter says that he saw the king's conquests in a vision and was blessing him when he looked at him. The musician is honored.

name: Flatterer always agrees with king even in opposite opinions; defense: he is king's servant.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: "High-born alone recognizes one of equal rank with himself." Jackal sees man with instrument he is unfamiliar with, comes up to him and salutes him "Lord of Delhi." Man calls him Lord of Jungle and tells above.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: HIMALAYA . Kumaun : NQ V No. 77 .
additional_description: Jackal sees man with instrument he is unfamiliar with, comes up to him and salutes him "Lord of Delhi." Man calls him Lord of Jungle and tells above.

name: Flattery of the wicked to escape death at his hands: "This is an offering to my lord Esau from his slave Jacob".
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Dissenting minister to king: "Let the king do as seems good in his eyes".
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Unpleasant truths must be withheld from the great.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

additional_description: King of apes asks visitors how they like his children (courtiers). Truthful visitor tells that they are very ugly, and is punished. Liar praises their beauty and receives reward.

name: Brother who conforms to naked people's customs honored. Two brothers go to the land of Naked People. One of the brothers wears clothes and is punished.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: Two brothers go to the land of Naked People. One of the brothers wears clothes and is punished.

name: Muddy bath prepared for blemished king.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Tact in reproving the great.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: Asks if there is harmony among the cities and republics of Greece: philosopher tells him to ask it of his own house.

name: King called baker's son: he has given the poet only loaves of bread. King sees jest and rewards poet.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: King sees jest and rewards poet.

name: King brought to sense of duty by woman's words. Drunken king sentences unjustly. Woman asks to appear before the king before he has dined. Her case is retried.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: Drunken king sentences unjustly. Woman asks to appear before the king before he has dined. Her case is retried.

name: Woman tactfully restrains amorous king.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: A soft answer turneth away wrath.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Man called a rogue by a nobleman makes a joke of the insult. He thus avoids trouble.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: He thus avoids trouble.

name: Physician willing to believe in four persons. Angers a theologian by disputing doctrine of the Trinity. "Don't get angry," he says; "rather than have you condemn me to hell, I would believe in four persons."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: Angers a theologian by disputing doctrine of the Trinity. "Don't get angry," he says; "rather than have you condemn me to hell, I would believe in four persons."

name: King makes absurd statement about flowers. Flatterer agrees: it is the king he serves, not the wretched flowers.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: GANGES VALLEY . Shyama - Shankar 79f
additional_description: Flatterer agrees: it is the king he serves, not the wretched flowers.

name: With a silent person one is alone. Angered bishop will not answer when addressed. Relents when priest says, "Since there is no one here I may heed the call of Nature."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: Angered bishop will not answer when addressed. Relents when priest says, "Since there is no one here I may heed the call of Nature."

name: Care in advising a king.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Yogi advises yogi blood for making king's leaky tank hold water: king cuts off yogi's head.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MADRAS . Telugu : Pantulu Ind . Antiq . XXVI 25

name: Keeping on good terms with hostile gods.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Dog asks raven why he sacrifices to Athene, since she hates raven because of his powers of augury. "The more reason to sacrifice. She will keep on good terms with me."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 61 (ET 231), 143 (ST 490); Halm Aesop No. 213.
additional_description: "The more reason to sacrifice. She will keep on good terms with me."

name: Man plays fool to protect himself in dealing with king.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Man pretends idiocy so as to avoid compromising himself when summoned to testify by two rival queens before the king.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Man recounts unpleasant happening to king when in good humor and draws laugh; rest draw punishment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: Dealing with the great – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.

name: The king and the cheap slippers. Steward buys the king a pair of slippers. King thinks not enough has been paid for them and refuses them. Steward buys another like the first and charges a good price. Learns that this is the way to deal with kings.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: Steward buys the king a pair of slippers. King thinks not enough has been paid for them and refuses them. Steward buys another like the first and charges a good price. Learns that this is the way to deal with kings.

name: Devil decides to leave of own accord. It is decided to drive the devil out of a man who is possessed. Devil sees the inevitable and flees so that they cannot say that he has been driven off.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J810. Policy in dealing with the great.
additional_description: It is decided to drive the devil out of a man who is possessed. Devil sees the inevitable and flees so that they cannot say that he has been driven off.

name: Vanquished ruler in disguise gets audience with victor. "What would you do if your enemy were to kneel before you and beg forgiveness?" "I would forgive him." Reveals identity and is pardoned.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: United cocks defeat partridge. When they quarrel they are defeated.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

additional_description: When they quarrel they are defeated.

name: Cranes fighting as allies defeat all enemies. If they quarrel they are doomed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: If they quarrel they are doomed.

name: Cats unite in battle against a wolf. They destroy wolf.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: They destroy wolf.

name: Self-dependence.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Grain will be cut when farmer attends to it himself. Lark leaves her young in the cornfield. They hear farmer tell sons to go to neighbors for help in harvesting. Lark tells young not to worry. Same when he sends for relatives. Farmer decides to harvest it himself. Larks move, for they now know that it will be done.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 867; Wienert FFC LVI *70 (ET 341), 126 (ST 351); Halm Aesop No. 210; Roumania: Schullerus FFC LXXVIII No. 93*.

name: Stag found by master when overlooked by servants. Hides under hay and escapes until master himself comes.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Hides under hay and escapes until master himself comes.

name: Gardener who plants vegetable tends it best.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Gods help those who help themselves. Ox driver must put his shoulder to the wheel before Hercules will help him.

additional_description: Ox driver must put his shoulder to the wheel before Hercules will help him.

name: Decisiveness of conduct.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Impossibility of pleasing everyone. One must act therefore without awaiting everyone's approval.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: One must act therefore without awaiting everyone's approval.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

additional_description: Attempt to please everyone with weather unavailing. Weather given therefore without regard to men's desires.

name: Miller, his son, and the ass: trying to please everyone. Miller blamed when he follows his son on foot; when he takes the son's place on the ass; when he takes the son behind him; and when he puts the son in front of him.

bibliographies: *Chauvin II 148 No 2, III 70, 145, VIII 140; *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 244 No. 541; Herbert III 420; *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 577; Scala Celi 135a No. 745; Alphabet No. 765. additional_description: Miller blamed when he follows his son on foot; when he takes the son's place on the ass; when he takes the son behind him; and when he puts the son in front of him.

name: Attention to warnings.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Warns man by loss of appetite, loss of desire for drink, and sickness. Man does not recognize the messengers.

name: Cranes disregard warnings and are killed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man disregards mother's warning and is punished.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man disregards priest's warning and is punished.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Miscellaneous aspects of wisdom.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Value depends upon real use.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Baldheaded man finds the comb: it is useless.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Foolish dog finds treasure and dies rather than leave it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Doctor unable to cure himself scorned.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Bald man tries to sell hair restorer. Scorned.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Scorned.

name: Pot calls kettle black.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Mother crab blames her children for not walking straight.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Drunk man makes sport of another man for being drunk.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Futility of trying to teach the stupid.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Results of labor lost in a moment of procrastination.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: After laboring to make a brazen head, artist loses labor in moment of procrastination.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man to be judged by his own qualities, not his clothes.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Guests strike man who tries to interfere in their quarrel. It is their host in old clothes. Guests are humiliated but forgiven.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: It is their host in old clothes. Guests are humiliated but forgiven.

name: Rich Brahmin wearing poor clothing is treated as a beggar.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Never use your entire resources.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man who never does any piece of work so well that he cannot improve it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Value of silence.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Parson's apology for not answering challenge: I have never regretted silence but have often regretted speech.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Philosopher does not join in with gossipers. He is silent because he fears that some day he may talk too much and not be able to keep silent.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: He is silent because he fears that some day he may talk too much and not be able to keep silent.

name: Man does not court ugly woman: she might say yes. Therefore he keeps silent.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Therefore he keeps silent.

name: Time renders all things commonplace.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Woman tests enduring power of gossip by having a servant ride through streets on a flayed ass. By the third day he has ceased to attract attention. She concludes that it will be the same way in connection with her intended marriage.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: By the third day he has ceased to attract attention. She concludes that it will be the same way in connection with her intended marriage.

name: Fox is terrified on first seeing lion. The third time she meets him she has no fear.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The third time she meets him she has no fear.

name: Futility of distant travel.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man desirous of traveling sent six miles to deliver a letter. Told that he has now seen the world, for he will never see more than valleys, hills, fields, meadows, water, streets, cities, and villages.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Told that he has now seen the world, for he will never see more than valleys, hills, fields, meadows, water, streets, cities, and villages.

name: Preciousness of untroubled sleep.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: King buys spendthrift's bed. It must have been an extraordinary bed to permit a man with so many debts to sleep on it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 503; Herbert III 128 No. 79, 170 No. 35; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: It must have been an extraordinary bed to permit a man with so many debts to sleep on it.

name: Futility of trying to hide an obvious deed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Horn will tell the tale. Thus answers goat to herdsman who begs her not to tell master that he has broken off her horn.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 72 (ET 367), 102 (ST 154); Halm Aesop No. 17.
additional_description: Thus answers goat to herdsman who begs her not to tell master that he has broken off her horn.

name: Money does not always bring happiness.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Type 754; *Toldo Zs. f. Vksk. XIII 420; *Crane Vitry 162 No. 66; Herbert III 281; Alphabet No. 276; Scala Celi 80b No. 459; Nouvelles Récréations No. 19; Chinese: Eberhard FFC CXX 258 No. 204.

name: [First Edition (Additions and Corrections): J1085.1[b]. Priest with crucifix and club. Orders church thieves to replace stolen goods. When crucifix does not bring obedience he forces them with club.]
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Possession of gold turns humble servant into arrogant one.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: King sees poor man far happier than himself.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Poor man returns to the emperor all save enough for comfort: wealth would take happiness from his simple life.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

section: J1140. Cleverness in detection of truth.

name: Detection of theft by finding bag-repairer. Two men are in a lawsuit and before witnesses seal the documents in a bag to await arrival of the king. One of the men cuts the bag open, changes the documents, and has a repairer sew it up again. On the king's arrival the change is discovered. By having a fine carpet repaired the expert repairer is discovered. Confession follows.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1140. Cleverness in detection of truth.
additional_description: Two men are in a lawsuit and before witnesses seal the documents in a bag to await arrival of the king. One of the men cuts the bag open, changes the documents, and has a repairer sew it up again. On the king's arrival the change is discovered. By having a fine carpet repaired the expert repairer is discovered. Confession follows.

name: Thief's money scales borrowed. A man buries gold and a thief steals it. The owner detects the criminal. He takes some money to the thief and borrows money scales "to weigh so as to bury with the other". The thief decides that he is detected and hastens to return the stolen money.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1140. Cleverness in detection of truth.
additional_description: A man buries gold and a thief steals it. The owner detects the criminal. He takes some money to the thief and borrows money scales "to weigh so as to bury with the other". The thief decides that he is detected and hastens to return the stolen money.

name: Thief suspected of crawling through hole must take off clothes. He is full of scratches and confesses.

section: J1140. Cleverness in detection of truth.
bibliographies: Jewish: bin Gorion Born Judas IV 99, 280.
additional_description: He is full of scratches and confesses.

division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1140. Cleverness in detection of truth.

name: Talkative wife discredited. Husband tells his talkative wife about treasure he has discovered. Husband may keep his treasure.

name: The woman with bad eyes. Physician called to doctor woman's eyes bandages them and then steals things each day. She refuses to pay fee and is haled to court. She says that her sight is worse than ever for whereas she used to see many things in her house she now sees very little. Theft is thus revealed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.

name: Bought behind the village. Lawyer declares unjustly that stolen horse has been bought and paid for. Angry farmer: "Yes, behind the village he bought and paid for it" (meaning that he stole it from the pasture). Lawyer pleads successfully that it is as well to buy a horse outside as inside the village.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.

Lawyer pleads successfully that it is as well to buy a horse outside as inside the village.

division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
additional_description: Knight attacks enemy contrary to orders. Part of army helps him and is killed. He disclaims responsibility since he had not asked their aid.

(3) Man falls from a bridge and kills boatsman's son: shall allow boatsman to fall from bridge and kill him.

division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.

name: The judge pays fine himself. Tires of the bickering of two men over a trifling sum.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
additional_description: Tires of the bickering of two men over a trifling sum.

name: Enoch Arden decision. Man believing wife dead becomes a priest. Later finds her alive. Pope's decision: he may remain both a priest and husband but must not consort with any other woman.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
bibliographies: Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 42; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: Man believing wife dead becomes a priest. Later finds her alive. Pope's decision: he may remain both a priest and husband but must not consort with any other woman.

name: Those who furnish equal number of animals for plow share equally in crop (in spite of amount of work done by each).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.

name: Actual rescuer gets woman. Watchers could never have got her merely by watching.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
additional_description: Watchers could never have got her merely by watching.

name: Cardinal's clever decision: that the monks who arise earliest may sound matins. There had been a great argument over this privilege.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
additional_description: There had been a great argument over this privilege.

name: Eye of king's foster-son damaged by sting of bee. Boy's friends demand eye of king's son in forfeit; king decrees instead destruction of swarm of bees so that guilty one may perish.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
additional_description: Boy's friends demand eye of king's son in forfeit; king decrees instead destruction of swarm of bees so that guilty one may perish.

name: Clever means of avoiding legal punishment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1180. Clever means of avoiding legal punishment.

name: Execution escaped by use of special permissions granted the condemned.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1180. Clever means of avoiding legal punishment.

name: Execution: man induced to kill self. King may not execute Brahmin, but gets him drunk and brings about his death.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1180. Clever means of avoiding legal punishment.
additional_description: King may not execute Brahmin, but gets him drunk and brings about his death.

name: Execution evaded by using three wishes. King ordains that guest who turns his plate shall be executed, but orders that anyone so condemned shall have three wishes granted. One of the wishes: to have all blinded who saw him turn the plate. He is freed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1180. Clever means of avoiding legal punishment.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Märchen des Mittelalters 230 No. 40; Herbert III 197; Oesterley No. 194.
additional_description: King ordains that guest who turns his plate shall be executed, but orders that anyone so condemned shall have three wishes granted. One of the wishes: to have all blinded who saw him turn the plate. He is freed.

name: Execution evaded by having three wishes granted: to be emperor, judge and the emperor's son-in-law during the last week of his life. As judge he frees himself and is in reality freed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1180. Clever means of avoiding legal punishment.
bibliographies: Köhler-Bolte II 651–57; Zs. f. Vksk. XV 222; Icelandic: Boberg.
additional_description: As judge he frees himself and is in reality freed.

name: Condemned man wins pardon by clever remark. Fool is allowed to jump off cliff (balcony) as punishment. Master expresses surprise that in three trials he has failed to hurl himself from the height. The jester offers the prince four trials. Amused prince pardons the jester.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.
section: J1180. Clever means of avoiding legal punishment.
additional_description: Fool is allowed to jump off cliff (balcony) as punishment. Master expresses surprise that in three trials he has failed to hurl himself from the height. The jester offers the prince four trials. Amused prince pardons the jester.

section: J1180. Clever means of avoiding legal punishment.

division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.

bibliographies: *Chauvin VI 63, 231; *Zachariae Zs f. Vksk. XXX–XXXII 50 n. 2; *Wesselski Arlotto II 215 No. 73; India: Thompson-Balys; Oceanic: *Dixon 199 n. 37; Africa (Angola): Chatelain 197 No. 26. Polynesia: Kirtley: Oceanic: *Dixon, 1916, 199n. 37.

name: Reductio ad absurdum: the decision about the colt. A man ties his mare to a second man's wagon. The mare bears a colt which the wagon-owner claims, saying that the wagon has borne a colt. Real owner of the colt shows the absurdity (1) by fishing in the street or (2) by telling that his wife is shooting fish in the garden. Neither of these things are so absurd as the decision.

division3: J1130–J1199. Cleverness in the law court.

Elwin ( 1949 ) ; Kurukh : Hahn 32 No. 17. - CEYLON . Parker I231f .; Köhler-Bolte I 460; *BP II 349ff., 371; *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 212 No. 434; India: *Thompson-Balys.
additional_description: A man ties his mare to a second man's wagon. The mare bears a colt which the wagon-owner claims, saying that the wagon has borne a colt. Real owner of the colt shows the absurdity (1) by fishing in the street or (2) by telling that his wife is shooting fish in the garden. Neither of these things are so absurd as the decision.

Priest hides and witnesses amorous intrigue between bishop and abbess, hearing bishop refer to the abbess's charms in biblical similes. Priest incorporates overheard references in his introduction to the Mass and is asked for an explanation. Bishop returns his fine.

additional_description: Ruler had instructed jesters to tease him. The poet cleverly answers his questioners pretending to have the impression that the questions are addressed to the ruler.

name: Division of the fat and lean fowls. Two fat fowls and one lean one at meal. Hero is lean, his two hosts fat. He is to divide. One lean fowl for the two fat people; two fat fowls for the one lean man.

name: Dividing the eggs: one man's hand in the pot at a time. Trickster alternates hands so fast that the other man never gets to put his hand in. He finally puts his foot in the pan.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1230–J1249. Clever dividing.
additional_description: Trickster alternates hands so fast that the other man never gets to put his hand in. He finally puts his foot in the pan.

division3: J1230–J1249. Clever dividing.

name: Hog's head divided according to scripture. To be divided among three students according to their skill in quoting. First: "And they cut one ear off" (takes ear). Second: "And they gave him a box on the ear" (takes other ear). Third: "And they took him away secretly" (takes whole hog away).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division3: J1230–J1249. Clever dividing.
additional_description: To be divided among three students according to their skill in quoting. First: "And they cut one ear off" (takes ear). Second: "And they gave him a box on the ear" (takes other ear). Third: "And they took him away secretly" (takes whole hog away).

name: Three men divide a large fish by quoting scripture.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

additional_description: By the great bugaboo! If I was the Almighty and had a moon, I'd hang it out on a night like this.

section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.

section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.

section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: Sick woman calls the parson but recovers meanwhile He insists on her taking the sacrament and charges for it. " Set it here on the table; perhaps I can sell it again.

section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: Priest offers to help peasant with "the body of the Lord".   " If God can be brought by a man, he is too weak to help me.

section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: Thief about to be hanged is told that if he truly repents he will eat Lord's Supper in heaven. " If that is true, won't you eat the supper for me; I'll reward you well.

section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: Says that he had his mind on God and did not remember what his body did. If those who criticized him had had their minds on God they would not have noticed it.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.

section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.

section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: King asks how much his funeral will cost. " Give me the three hundred ducats now and when I am dead throw me into the Tiber.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: At his mother's funeral a boy takes the money laid on the altar for masses. When the priest objects, the boy says, "When your mother dies you may take the money too.

name: God in the puddle. A Jew objects to the doctrine that God could exist in the Virgin Mary. A disputant asks if he believes God is everywhere; then if God is in a mud puddle. The Jew agrees. The disputant condemns the Jew for believing that God could exist in a puddle and not in a pure virgin.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

bibliographies: Parson: "God gives it to you." – Sexton: "Now a man is coming with half a hog on his shoulder." – Parson: "God gives it to me." Type 1829*; Russian: Andrejev No. 1829*.
additional_description: During the sermon the parson bids the sexton see if anyone is coming. The sexton: "A man is coming with a wheel on his shoulder" Parson: "God gives it to you." Sexton: "Now a man is coming with half a hog on his shoulder." Parson: "God gives it to me."

name: Man absents self from church because he does not like to hear people slandered.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.

name: Would be first in all things. Chaplain accused of being first to start all knavery thus defends himself.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: Chaplain accused of being first to start all knavery thus defends himself.

name: Scolding priest says he is merely trying to get even for all the scolding he must undergo.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.

name: Transmutation of the quail. Bishop brought quail on Friday orders them cooked. Blamed. If he can turn bread into the body of the Lord why can he not turn quail into fish?
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
bibliographies: Frey (ed. Bolte) 247 No. 85; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 100; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: Bishop brought quail on Friday orders them cooked. Blamed. If he can turn bread into the body of the Lord why can he not turn quail into fish?

name: The capon and the hen. Bishop refuses a favor to an abbess on the ground that he does not love her. Abbess: "I can well believe that. The capon never loves the hen."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
bibliographies: Wesselski Mönchslatein No. 59; Mensa Philosophica No. 115.
additional_description: Bishop refuses a favor to an abbess on the ground that he does not love her. Abbess: "I can well believe that. The capon never loves the hen."

name: Praying before the King of Kings. Man while praying refuses to salute superior. While standing before the King of Kings he could not show respect to inferiors.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
bibliographies: Gaster Exempla 194 No. 45; Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 617.
additional_description: Man while praying refuses to salute superior. While standing before the King of Kings he could not show respect to inferiors.

name: Robber's defense for stealing from rich. God will not permit them to enter heaven unless we take their ill-gotten goods from them.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: God will not permit them to enter heaven unless we take their ill-gotten goods from them.

name: Who bore the Savior on his back? (St. Christopher.) Parishioner: "The ass, for he bore both the son and his mother."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: (St. Christopher.) Parishioner: "The ass, for he bore both the son and his mother."

name: Mice die of hunger. Complaints against underpaid priest that he does not stay at home. Says he cannot stay at home for the mice are dying of hunger since he receives only forty florins a year.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: Complaints against underpaid priest that he does not stay at home. Says he cannot stay at home for the mice are dying of hunger since he receives only forty florins a year.

name: Their own quarters need it more. Such is the answer given monks who insist that quarters occupied by the king and his train be reconsecrated.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: Such is the answer given monks who insist that quarters occupied by the king and his train be reconsecrated.

name: Youth announces fire in imitation of priest's metaphorical language. The fire set by cat's tail gains headway.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
additional_description: The fire set by cat's tail gains headway.

name: Cannot leave court of God: reply of saint when king invites him to court.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1260. Repartee based on church or clergy.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Temple Legends III 325 .

name: Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.

name: Eunuch visits augurer to see whether he is to be a father. "When I look at the livers, you are to be a father. When I look at you, I see that you are not even a man."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: "When I look at the livers, you are to be a father. When I look at you, I see that you are not even a man."

name: The gift of the fool. Of three brothers the shoemaker makes shoes for the queen and princess; the tailor, clothes; the fool – children.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: Of three brothers the shoemaker makes shoes for the queen and princess; the tailor, clothes; the fool children.

name: Children by day and by night. Artist paints beautiful children but his own are ugly. One kind, he says, are made by day, the other by night.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: Artist paints beautiful children but his own are ugly. One kind, he says, are made by day, the other by night.

name: His father has been in Rome. A young man comes to Rome who looks like the emperor. Latter asks him if he mother has ever been in Rome. No, but my father has been here often. (Not son but brother.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 502; Mensa Philosophica No. 3; Nouvelles Récréations No. 15; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: A young man comes to Rome who looks like the emperor. Latter asks him if he mother has ever been in Rome. No, but my father has been here often. (Not son but brother.)

name: The child born too soon.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.

name: Child born one month after marriage. Father-in-law to son-in-law: "Do like my grandson and you will always be ahead of others."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: Father-in-law to son-in-law: "Do like my grandson and you will always be ahead of others."

name: Too much for his income. Child born two months after marriage. Husband returns wife to her father (uncle) saying: "I can't afford a child every two months."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: Child born two months after marriage. Husband returns wife to her father (uncle) saying: "I can't afford a child every two months."

name: Child born on wedding night. Groom leaves. Can't afford a child every time he lies with his wife.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
bibliographies: Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 29; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: Groom leaves. Can't afford a child every time he lies with his wife.

name: Repartee regarding the parentage of children – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.

name: Plea for a good father. Mother of twelve on deathbed explains to family that not all her children are legitimate. Gives the paternity of each child. Youngest leaves his food to say: "Please, mother, give me a good father!" (Cf. J325.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: Mother of twelve on deathbed explains to family that not all her children are legitimate. Gives the paternity of each child. Youngest leaves his food to say: "Please, mother, give me a good father!"

name: Even God can overdo it! Returning sailor finds his hovel transformed into a fine house. "Where does all this come from?" Wife: "God's bounty." Illegitimate child greets mother. Same question, same answer. Sailor: "I don't want God to help me so much!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: Returning sailor finds his hovel transformed into a fine house. "Where does all this come from?" Wife: "God's bounty." Illegitimate child greets mother. Same question, same answer. Sailor: "I don't want God to help me so much!"

name: Suitor hesitates to marry girl as immature (or too delicate). Girl's father protests, saying that she has had three children.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: Girl's father protests, saying that she has had three children.

name: In numbers there is strength. The children of the Genoese are strong because there is more help.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1270. Repartee concerning the parentage of children.
additional_description: The children of the Genoese are strong because there is more help.

name: Repartee with ruler (judge, etc.).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: "If I were a tyrant you would not say so." Answer made by emperor to one who calls him a tyrant.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Answer made by emperor to one who calls him a tyrant.

name: Trickster chooses his gift. To be rewarded by ruler with a coin, an ass, a sheep, or a vineyard. Answers that he will take the coin, mount the ass, drive the sheep into the vineyard, and there pray for the ruler.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: To be rewarded by ruler with a coin, an ass, a sheep, or a vineyard. Answers that he will take the coin, mount the ass, drive the sheep into the vineyard, and there pray for the ruler.

name: Gifts from the brothers. A king gives a man a coin. "Is that all you give your brother?" "Are you my brother?" "Surely, we both pray, Our father, etc." "If all your brothers give you as much as I you will be rich."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A king gives a man a coin. "Is that all you give your brother?" "Are you my brother?" "Surely, we both pray, Our father, etc." "If all your brothers give you as much as I you will be rich."

name: Bird overcareful about food suitable to its color is killed by eagle.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Do not leave it to your successor. A widow stops a ruler on his way to war. He may be killed and he should not leave the act of justice as a credit to his successor.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A widow stops a ruler on his way to war. He may be killed and he should not leave the act of justice as a credit to his successor.

name: Show me how it is done. Wronged woman thus addresses ruler, who is indifferent to insults directed at him. She wishes to be shown how to bear insults. This rebukes him.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Wronged woman thus addresses ruler, who is indifferent to insults directed at him. She wishes to be shown how to bear insults. This rebukes him.

name: Cease being a king. A widow asks justice of a king. The latter says that he is too busy to hear her. "Then cease being a king," replies the widow. Her bold reply wins an audience with the king.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A widow asks justice of a king. The latter says that he is too busy to hear her. "Then cease being a king," replies the widow. Her bold reply wins an audience with the king.

name: Against his will. A thief condemned to the gallows tells the king, "I do what you do and it is against my will." King: "You shall also be hanged against your will."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A thief condemned to the gallows tells the king, "I do what you do and it is against my will." King: "You shall also be hanged against your will."

name: His proper title. A peasant goes to a judge and thinking to gain his favor addresses him with high titles. The judge calls him a fool. "I was mistaken, you swine!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A peasant goes to a judge and thinking to gain his favor addresses him with high titles. The judge calls him a fool. "I was mistaken, you swine!"

name: Repartee with ruler (judge, etc.) – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Not a locksmith. A judge asks a pseudo-prophet to prove his powers by opening a difficult lock. "I am a prophet, not a locksmith."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A judge asks a pseudo-prophet to prove his powers by opening a difficult lock. "I am a prophet, not a locksmith."

name: Bishop and prince. Peasant tells bishop, who rides by with forty horses, that he wonders if St. Kilian at Würzburg is also riding with forty horses. Bishop excuses extravagance by saying that he is also a prince and that it is the prince, not the bishop, who is using the horses. "If the prince should become a fool, what would the bishop do then?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Peasant tells bishop, who rides by with forty horses, that he wonders if St. Kilian at W'rzburg is also riding with forty horses. Bishop excuses extravagance by saying that he is also a prince and that it is the prince, not the bishop, who is using the horses. "If the prince should become a fool, what would the bishop do then?"

name: Will not try to correct them. Pope calls persistent courtier a fool. Latter says that there are people who call the pope the same thing and that he for one will not try to correct them.
