chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Pope calls persistent courtier a fool. Latter says that there are people who call the pope the same thing and that he for one will not try to correct them.

name: The needy philosopher. Philosopher asks ruler for money Ruler says that philosophers do not need money. Philosopher: "If I am rewarded, I will say that you are right; I will no longer need money."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Philosopher asks ruler for money Ruler says that philosophers do not need money. Philosopher: "If I am rewarded, I will say that you are right; I will no longer need money."

name: Wearing all his clothes. Shivering king (rich man) to tattered peasant: "Aren't you cold?" Peasant: "No, if you wore all your clothes as I do, you wouldn't be cold either!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Shivering king (rich man) to tattered peasant: "Aren't you cold?" Peasant: "No, if you wore all your clothes as I do, you wouldn't be cold either!"

name: The prince's excuse. King to son who has been accused of rape: "I never did anything like that." Prince: "Your father was not king!" King: "Your son will never be one if you keep that up!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: King to son who has been accused of rape: "I never did anything like that." Prince: "Your father was not king!" King: "Your son will never be one if you keep that up!"

name: Shoemaker speaks ill of lord's rule. Lord takes his tools away from him. Shoemaker begs for them saying that he cannot carry on his business without them. Ruler: "I thought ruling was your business so I took your tools to learn shoemaking."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Lord takes his tools away from him. Shoemaker begs for them saying that he cannot carry on his business without them. Ruler: "I thought ruling was your business so I took your tools to learn shoemaking."

name: One ear saved for other litigant. Judge stops up one ear while first litigant presents his case. He is saving one ear for the second litigant.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Judge stops up one ear while first litigant presents his case. He is saving one ear for the second litigant.

name: Seek harmony in your own house. King brought to sense of duty by philosopher who tells him to seek harmony in his own house before inquiring about the harmony in his kingdom.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: King brought to sense of duty by philosopher who tells him to seek harmony in his own house before inquiring about the harmony in his kingdom.

name: King cannot destroy the city. A philosopher of the city came to him asking mercy for it King said he would do nothing he asked. Philosopher then asked him to destroy the city. This saves the city.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A philosopher of the city came to him asking mercy for it King said he would do nothing he asked. Philosopher then asked him to destroy the city. This saves the city.

name: Man, fined for sabbath-breaking, asks for receipt, explains that if God asks for it, he will not have to journey to hell to get it from the judge.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man is arrested for drunkenness; he is so drunk that trial must be postponed. When he is tried later, he is told how at the earlier trial he had kept repeating that the judge was a very wise judge. When he hears this he admits that he must have been very drunk.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: When he is tried later, he is told how at the earlier trial he had kept repeating that the judge was a very wise judge. When he hears this he admits that he must have been very drunk.

name: Weaver hearing of tax for every doorway of weavers takes his door to the khan: will not return for the sidewalls of his house.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Swynnerton Upper Indus 45 .

name: Gem offered by monarch to one who can first go around the kingdom. Trickster goes around king and says king is kingdom.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Trickster goes around king and says king is kingdom.

name: Thief serves king buffaloes he has killed in hunt and lost; thus reproaches king's wastefulness.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: HIMALAYA . Lepcha : Stocks 385 No. 22 .

name: Hairless palms from giving and receiving gold. Jester flatters prince for bounty.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Jester flatters prince for bounty.

name: Emperor: "What people have the biggest bellies?" Jester answers: "The landlords."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Jester answers: "The landlords."

name: Holy man asked by king for the heart of religion answers: "You are sitting on your throne and I'm sitting on the ground, so how can I tell."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Tailor caught resting his head on royal robe while he rests tells king there is no better resting place for king's robe, for "the head is the king of the body."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: King to smoker: "Even donkeys will have nothing to do with tobacco." Smoker: "Donkeys don't know how to enjoy themselves."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: GANGES VALLEY . Shyama- Shankar 78 .
additional_description: Smoker: "Donkeys don't know how to enjoy themselves."

name: Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.

name: Question answered by absurd counterquestion.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.

name: Black beans, white soup. Question: How can black beans make a white soup? Answer: How can a white whip on the naked flesh make black welts.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: Question: How can black beans make a white soup? Answer: How can a white whip on the naked flesh make black welts.

name: Why is it that black cow eats green grass, gives white milk and yellow butter? Answer: The same reason blackberries are red when they are green.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: Answer: The same reason blackberries are red when they are green.

name: Theological questions answered by propounding simple questions in science. Where was God before he made heaven and earth? and the like answered by "Why a louse bite raises a blister, a flee bite raises a swelling, and a gnat bite is unnoticeable?" If you cannot answer such simple questions how can you pry into God's secrets.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: Where was God before he made heaven and earth? and the like answered by "Why a louse bite raises a blister, a flee bite raises a swelling, and a gnat bite is unnoticeable?" If you cannot answer such simple questions how can you pry into God's secrets.

name: How many priests should one have in one place? How many fox tails will reach to heaven? All depends on the length of the tails.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: How many fox tails will reach to heaven? All depends on the length of the tails.

name: How much cloth would it take to make God's coat? Just as much as for me, for what you have done for a poor person in my name you have done for me.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: Just as much as for me, for what you have done for a poor person in my name you have done for me.

name: "In this sesame flower where is the oil?" "When your mother conceived where were you?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: "When your mother conceived where were you?"

name: Tide inquires whether moon is up. Minnow seeing absurdity of question (since tide could not be up without the moon) tells the tide to wait till he gets a drink and he will tell.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: Minnow seeing absurdity of question (since tide could not be up without the moon) tells the tide to wait till he gets a drink and he will tell.

name: Reductio ad absurdum of proposal.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.

name: Little bird as large bird's mate. A large bird wishes to mate with a little bird. The latter says that she is going to swallow a large eel. The large bird sees the absurdity of his proposal.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: A large bird wishes to mate with a little bird. The latter says that she is going to swallow a large eel. The large bird sees the absurdity of his proposal.

name: Absurdity: sea (river) is on fire. Not more absurd than crow claiming swan as wife.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: Not more absurd than crow claiming swan as wife.

name: If his head is taken off other punishments do not matter. Judge shows criminal mercy: he will not punish him as he deserves, only take off his head.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: Judge shows criminal mercy: he will not punish him as he deserves, only take off his head.

name: Turning king into Brahmin like turning donkey into horse.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.

name: Pot full of milk as sign that city is full of fakirs; flower on top of milk not disturbing it as sign that one more will not matter.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: U.P. Oudh : Crooke Ind . Antiq . XXIX 402 .

name: All appurtenances included. Butcher buyer demands saddle and ornaments along with camel (or the like). Seller later buys all heads in butcher shop: demands heads of butcher's family.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1290. Reductio ad absurdum of question or proposal.
additional_description: Butcher buyer demands saddle and ornaments along with camel (or the like). Seller later buys all heads in butcher shop: demands heads of butcher's family.

name: Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.

name: How he shall be mourned. Women insist on knowing how a man wants to be mourned when he dies. "Mourn me as a man who was tormented by women talking foolishness."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
additional_description: Women insist on knowing how a man wants to be mourned when he dies. "Mourn me as a man who was tormented by women talking foolishness."

name: The overloaded mule. Priest complains that miller's mule is overloaded. "No, he isn't; he can still carry all your and your brothers' patience."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
additional_description: Priest complains that miller's mule is overloaded. "No, he isn't; he can still carry all your and your brothers' patience."

name: Aesop with the lantern. Aesop goes for fire to a neighbor's in the daytime and lights a lantern so as to bring the fire back. Fool asks him what he is hunting for with the lantern in the daytime. "I seek a man" (not a busybody).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
additional_description: Aesop goes for fire to a neighbor's in the daytime and lights a lantern so as to bring the fire back. Fool asks him what he is hunting for with the lantern in the daytime. "I seek a man" (not a busybody).

name: Why the black clothes. A man goes forth in black clothes. People are curious as to the reason. "I am wearing mourning for the father of my son."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
additional_description: A man goes forth in black clothes. People are curious as to the reason. "I am wearing mourning for the father of my son."

name: How the tail pointed. One who believes in auguries asks peasant woman if she has seen a bird. "Yes, a crow." And in what direction was his tail pointing?" Answer: "Toward the rear!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
additional_description: One who believes in auguries asks peasant woman if she has seen a bird. "Yes, a crow." And in what direction was his tail pointing? " Answer: "Toward the rear! "

name: How marriage was consummated. Mother asks newly-wedded daughter if she approached her husband the first night. Answer: "He approached me."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
additional_description: Mother asks newly-wedded daughter if she approached her husband the first night. Answer: "He approached me.

name: Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.

name: Man asks naked Indian if he is not cold. Indian asks if man's face is cold. Man replies that it is not. Indian replies: "Me all face!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.

name: "How often do you kill your ducks?" Answer: "Only once."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
additional_description: "  Answer: "Only once.

name: "Where did dirt go when canal was dug?" Disgusted father-in-law: "I have eaten half and your father half, to have such a fool son-in-law."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1300. Officiousness or foolish questions rebuked.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: BENGAL . Grierson Survey V ( 1 ) 301 .
additional_description: "  Disgusted father-in-law: "I have eaten half and your father half, to have such a fool son-in-law.

name: Repartee concerning wine.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: What is wanted, not what is asked. A servant is so trained that when the host asks for wine from a good cask he brings it from a cheap one. When the guest objects, the host says that the servant brought not what was asked for but what was wanted.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A servant is so trained that when the host asks for wine from a good cask he brings it from a cheap one. When the guest objects, the host says that the servant brought not what was asked for but what was wanted.

name: The watered wine.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Serve the water and the wine separately. Server of diluted wine thus taunted.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Server of diluted wine thus taunted.

name: Washed in the Rhine. Server of diluted Rhine wine thus taunted.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Server of diluted Rhine wine thus taunted.

name: Baptized wine. Christ's making wine from water has made all landlords try to do it too. Man prefers Jewish wine to the baptized kind.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Christ's making wine from water has made all landlords try to do it too. Man prefers Jewish wine to the baptized kind.

name: Old wine. Man given his choice of old or new wine says, "We must honor old age."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man given his choice of old or new wine says, "We must honor old age.

name: The road to Heaven. To doctor: "If this is the way to get to Heaven (drinking) I don't want to know any other!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: To doctor: "If this is the way to get to Heaven (drinking) I don't want to know any other!

name: Points of view. Man to friend who drinks very little: "If everyone drank as you do, wine would be cheap." Answer: "On the contrary, it would be expensive because I drink all I want."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man to friend who drinks very little: "If everyone drank as you do, wine would be cheap. " Answer: "On the contrary, it would be expensive because I drink all I want.

name: Very small to be so old. Guest criticizes host's small serving of wine which he said was six years old.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Guest criticizes host's small serving of wine which he said was six years old.

name: Small jug preferred. Man asks that large jug be filled with wine. Is told to go to the river. Is accommodated when he presents small jug. (Cf. L251.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man asks that large jug be filled with wine. Is told to go to the river. Is accommodated when he presents small jug.

name: Wine gives ambassador courage to address Pope. "Another drink and I could have slapped his face!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Another drink and I could have slapped his face!

name: Repartee concerning wine – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man says: "I do love my enemies and I have great affection for them that hurt me." (Enemies are rum and hard cider.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: "  (Enemies are rum and hard cider.)

name: Repartee concerning drunkenness.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The unrepentant drunkard.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Where did he get the wine? Father shows drunkard son a drunk man being mocked on the street. Instead of taking it as a warning the son says "Where does one get such good wine?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) Nos. 21, 814; Italian Novella: Rotunda; Jewish: bin Gorion Born Judas II 115, 344, *Neuman.
additional_description: Father shows drunkard son a drunk man being mocked on the street. Instead of taking it as a warning the son says "Where does one get such good wine?

name: Though old woman is made to believe she is in hell she calls for drinking companions.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The great thirst.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Will take care of the thirst. Doctors consult how to rid fever-stricken drunkard of his great thirst. "You take care of the fever; I shall take care of the thirst."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Arlotto II 267 No. 217; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: Doctors consult how to rid fever-stricken drunkard of his great thirst. " You take care of the fever; I shall take care of the thirst.

name: Sleeping on salt. Priest blamed for large amount of wine he drinks tells people to consider his great thirst. He has slept on a sack of salt and has enough thirst for a week.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Priest blamed for large amount of wine he drinks tells people to consider his great thirst. He has slept on a sack of salt and has enough thirst for a week.

name: Should have brought him drink. Drunkard's wife takes him when he is drunk to a tomb and, masking as a ghost brings him food. "If you had known me better you would have brought me drink."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 38; Halm Aesop No. 108.
additional_description: Drunkard's wife takes him when he is drunk to a tomb and, masking as a ghost brings him food. " If you had known me better you would have brought me drink.

name: Stung by the goblet. Doctor tells drunkard, "The goblet has stung you." "If I had known that I would have drunk out of a glass."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Doctor tells drunkard, "The goblet has stung you. " "If I had known that I would have drunk out of a glass.

name: Repartee concerning beggars.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Persistent beggar invited upstairs. A beggar will not come in but insists on the man coming down to the door. When he asks alms the man bids him come upstairs. Then he says he has nothing for him. "You made me come down for nothing; I make you come up for nothing."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A beggar will not come in but insists on the man coming down to the door. When he asks alms the man bids him come upstairs. Then he says he has nothing for him. " You made me come down for nothing; I make you come up for nothing.

name: Beggar wants bread. A numskull tells his mother that a beggar is below asking for bread. Mother: "Tell him I am not at home." Numskull: "He doesn't want you, he wants bread."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A numskull tells his mother that a beggar is below asking for bread. Mother: "Tell him I am not at home. " Numskull: "He doesn't want you, he wants bread.

name: Prove me a liar. Beggar: "Good day, you stingy fellows." They: "We are not stingy fellows." Beggar: "Then give and prove me a liar."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Beggar: "Good day, you stingy fellows. " They: "We are not stingy fellows. " Beggar: "Then give and prove me a liar.

name: Beggar tells stingy to go beg. They say they have no meat, no bread, no wine, etc. "Then go beg; you have more need than I."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: They say they have no meat, no bread, no wine, etc. "Then go beg; you have more need than I."

name: Beggar claims to be emperor's brother. (All men are descended from Adam.) Emperor gives him small coin. Beggar protests. Emperor: "If all your brothers gave you that much you would be richer than I."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: (All men are descended from Adam.) Emperor gives him small coin. Beggar protests. Emperor: "If all your brothers gave you that much you would be richer than I." Italian Novella: Rotunda.

name: Asking costs nothing. So says beggar who asks goldsmith to plate his club with gold.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: So says beggar who asks goldsmith to plate his club with gold.

name: Retorts from hungry persons.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Retort from underfed servant (child).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Softening bread-crusts. An avaricious master feeds bread-crusts to his servants. "The crusts are already getting soft."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: An avaricious master feeds bread-crusts to his servants. " The crusts are already getting soft.

name: Asking the large fish. Parents serve boy a small fish and keep back a large one for themselves. Knowing this, the boy puts the fish to his ear. He says that he has asked the fish a question; the fish cannot answer but tells him to ask the large fish under the bed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 247 No. 158; *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 700; Lithuanian: Balys Index No. *1565; Italian Novella: Rotunda; L. Schmidt Oesterr. Zs. f. Vksk. 1954, 134.
additional_description: Parents serve boy a small fish and keep back a large one for themselves. Knowing this, the boy puts the fish to his ear. He says that he has asked the fish a question; the fish cannot answer but tells him to ask the large fish under the bed.

name: A dog to scent the rice. Given very thin rice soup, wit inquires about the master's dogs. "He should have one to scent the rice in this soup."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 259 No 206, Arlotto II 75 No. 105.
additional_description: Given very thin rice soup, wit inquires about the master's dogs. " He should have one to scent the rice in this soup.

name: Two eggs. Widow serves tailor one egg. He sings, "One egg, one egg." She decides one egg is not enough and serves him two next time. He then sings "Two eggs are two eggs." He is next given two eggs and a sausage, etc.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Widow serves tailor one egg. He sings, "One egg, one egg. " She decides one egg is not enough and serves him two next time. He then sings "Two eggs are two eggs. " He is next given two eggs and a sausage, etc.

name: Hungry apprentice attracts master's attention by telling lies on him.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Spanish: Boggs FFC XC 143 No. 1718, Keller.

name: Hungry shepherd attracts attention. He tells of a cow with four teats who bore five calves. They ask what the fifth calf does while the other four are nursing. "It looks on just as I am doing now."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: He tells of a cow with four teats who bore five calves. They ask what the fifth calf does while the other four are nursing. " It looks on just as I am doing now.

name: Stingy innkeeper cured of serving weak beer. She always gives the servants a pitcher of weak beer before meals so as to fill them up. One of them: "I wash out my insides so as to have more room for food." She changes her practice.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: She always gives the servants a pitcher of weak beer before meals so as to fill them up. One of them: "I wash out my insides so as to have more room for food. " She changes her practice.

name: The little lumps of sugar are sweeter, says the hostess. The servant says that he doesn't like sweets and takes the large lumps.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The servant says that he doesn't like sweets and takes the large lumps.

name: Hungry son gets cherries. He slaps another son, and explains that the other boy was saying that he would not get any of the father's cherries. The father shares the cherries.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: He slaps another son, and explains that the other boy was saying that he would not get any of the father's cherries. The father shares the cherries.

name: Hungry student gets meat. By telling a mewing cat that it could not yet have the bones because no meat has been served him, a collegian calls attention to an oversight on the part of a servant.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: By telling a mewing cat that it could not yet have the bones because no meat has been served him, a collegian calls attention to an oversight on the part of a servant.

name: Hired men sing of displeasure with food; change song when food is improved (cante fable). The Westmoreland text follows: (Mowers sing this very slowly, mow in tempo)|Curds and whey, Iv-ve-ry day,|(After the food is improved they sing and work in tempo)|Ham and eggs, mind thy legs. (Cf. K1546.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The Westmoreland text follows:

name: Hired man shows in saying grace how better food has resulted from arrival of unexpected guests. Nebraska text: O Lord of Love who art above|Thy blessings have descended:|Biscuits and tea for supper I see|When mush and milk was intended.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Prayer over the underdone hen. A guest is seen uttering a prayer before an underdone hen at the table. "She must be a goddess turned into a hen, for she has been over the fire and spared."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A guest is seen uttering a prayer before an underdone hen at the table. " She must be a goddess turned into a hen, for she has been over the fire and spared.

name: The liking for food and drink.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The best music. What music do you like best? The sound of plates and spoons.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: What music do you like best? The sound of plates and spoons.

name: Before, during, and after. A priest, asked when he would have certain choice wine served, before or after the meal, replies, "The holy Mary was Virgin before, during, and after the birth."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A priest, asked when he would have certain choice wine served, before or after the meal, replies, "The holy Mary was Virgin before, during, and after the birth.

name: Unwelcome guest tells about the hidden food. Having seen his hostess hide it, he tells about it in the form of a tale.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Italian: Basile Pentamerone II No. 10; India: Thompson-Balys: INDIA . Venkataswami 81f .; Japanese: Ikeda.
additional_description: Having seen his hostess hide it, he tells about it in the form of a tale.

name: Why he did not eat the bread. When host inquires, he replies, "If thou be the son of God, command that these stones be made bread."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: When host inquires, he replies, "If thou be the son of God, command that these stones be made bread.

name: Maid rebukes pilgrim for eating too much. "If both of us had been present at the miracle of the loaves and fishes you would have eaten me too!" Pilgrim: "I wouldn't have eaten you but I would have chewed on you a bit!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 83; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: If both of us had been present at the miracle of the loaves and fishes you would have eaten me too! " Pilgrim: "I wouldn't have eaten you but I would have chewed on you a bit! " Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 83; Italian Novella:

name: Rude retorts.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Women call each other prostitutes.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Prostitutes wander. A woman having given her cook leave of absence for the next day asks her what day it is. "Saturday." "No, it is the day on which the prostitutes wander." The cook: "Yes, from one prostitute to another. Today I am with you, tomorrow with your sister."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A woman having given her cook leave of absence for the next day asks her what day it is. " Saturday. " "No, it is the day on which the prostitutes wander. " The cook: "Yes, from one prostitute to another. Today I am with you, tomorrow with your sister.

name: The envious accuser. A woman accuses another of being a harlot. The second: "You would like to be in my place but no one wants you."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A woman accuses another of being a harlot. The second: "You would like to be in my place but no one wants you.

name: Person calls another an ass.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The burden of two asses. A king and his son hunting on a hot day put their fur coats on the fool's back. King: "You have an ass's load on you." Fool: "Rather the burdens of two asses."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 239 No. 527; India: Thompson-Balys: HIMALAYA . Upreti Proverbs 16 , 281 .
additional_description: A king and his son hunting on a hot day put their fur coats on the fool's back. King: "You have an ass's load on you. " Fool: "Rather the burdens of two asses.

name: Age is relative. Young rival derides old one for his age. Oldster: "An ass of twenty is older than a man of seventy."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Young rival derides old one for his age. Oldster: "An ass of twenty is older than a man of seventy.

name: Whom it concerns. There is someone carrying a goose. How does that concern me? He is carrying it to your house. How does that concern you?
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: There is someone carrying a goose. How does that concern me? He is carrying it to your house. How does that concern you?

name: Not in his line of business. At market a man enquires of another: "How is the moon, three-quarters or full?" "I don't know. I have neither bought nor sold one."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: At market a man enquires of another: "How is the moon, three-quarters or full? " "I don't know. I have neither bought nor sold one.

name: The one exception. "You are a good man; there is not your equal on the earth. You have everything for yourself alone; only your wife is public property."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: You are a good man; there is not your equal on the earth. You have everything for yourself alone; only your wife is public property.

name: The flatterer's retort. Two men meet a homely girl. One of them: "Who wouldn't call that girl pretty?" The girl overhears and says, "No one would say it of you." The man: "Anyone might say it who would lie as I have lied about you."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Two men meet a homely girl. One of them: "Who wouldn't call that girl pretty? " The girl overhears and says, "No one would say it of you. " The man: "Anyone might say it who would lie as I have lied about you.

name: Ancient and modern ancestors. To a prince who boasted that he was descended from the Trojans a doctor replies: "My people are of Nurenberg. Everyone knows who they are; but of the Trojans no one knows anything except that Aeneas was a traitor and Romulus a robber."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: To a prince who boasted that he was descended from the Trojans a doctor replies: "My people are of Nurenberg. Everyone knows who they are; but of the Trojans no one knows anything except that Aeneas was a traitor and Romulus a robber.

name: No thanks to the messenger. A messenger tells a man that he has a newborn son. "Thanks are to God, but I am not beholden to you for it."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A messenger tells a man that he has a newborn son. " Thanks are to God, but I am not beholden to you for it.

name: Monk says that he is a stallion. In reply to women's taunts he boasts of his powers. A woman calls on the devil to come and ride him.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: In reply to women's taunts he boasts of his powers. A woman calls on the devil to come and ride him.

name: Too late for the same advice. Impoverished spendthrift sarcastically to thrifty person: "Stop spending so freely!" "It's too late to give you the same advice."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Impoverished spendthrift sarcastically to thrifty person: "Stop spending so freely! " "It's too late to give you the same advice.

name: To be rewarded by his kind. Unworthy person is rewarded. Asks worthy one: "Why is it that I am rewarded and you are not?" Answer: "Because you have found more of your kind than I have of mine." (Also told of Dante and a minstrel.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Unworthy person is rewarded. Asks worthy one: "Why is it that I am rewarded and you are not? " Answer: "Because you have found more of your kind that I have of mine. " (Also told of Dante and a minstrel.)

name: Rude retorts – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man decides to spend 100 florins to become known. Friend: "You had better spend 200 so as to not be found out."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Friend: "You had better spend 200 so as to not be found out.

name: The prodigy's retort. An old man says of a youthful prodigy: "He will be an idiot in old age because perfection before maturity brings on deterioration of the mind." Youth: "You must have been very wise in your youth!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: An old man says of a youthful prodigy: "He will be an idiot in old age because perfection before maturity brings on deterioration of the mind. " Youth: "You must have been very wise in your youth!

name: Two men meet in narrow passage. One says: "I do not give every coxcomb the wall!" The other says: "I do, sir!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: One says: "I do not give every coxcomb the wall! " The other says: "I do, sir!

name: Person asks: "Whose fool are you?" Answer: "I am the Bishop of Durham's fool; whose fool are you?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: " Answer: "I am the Bishop of Durham's fool; whose fool are you?

name: Why soldier is silent before king: is always so when questioned by stupid person. [Inadvertant duplication of J1714.5.]
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: BOMBAY . Marathi : Dexter 163 No. 46 .

name: Cynical retorts concerning honesty.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The account-book of mistakes. A king, hearing that a man keeps an account-book of people's mistakes asks to see about his own record. He reads that the king has made a mistake in trusting a certain sum of money to a servant. King: "How if he comes back with it?" "I shall cross off your name and put him down for making a mistake."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Arlotto I 181 No. 5; Chauvin II 153 No. 20; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: A king, hearing that a man keeps an account-book of people's mistakes asks to see about his own record. He reads that the king has made a mistake in trusting a certain sum of money to a servant. King: "How if he comes back with it? " "I shall cross off your name and put him down for making a mistake.

name: Safe since no white man is near. White man asks Indian if he can safely leave some of his belongings inside the Indian's lodge. The Indian assures him that he can: "There is no white man within a hundred miles of here." (Cf. X600.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: White man asks Indian if he can safely leave some of his belongings inside the Indian's lodge. The Indian assures him that he can: "There is no white man within a hundred miles of here.

name: Retorts concerning debts.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Where you got it last year. Parson tells borrower to get the corn at the same place as he got it last year. There is none there. "Then you didn't return it as you said you would, and there is none to lend you this year."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Arlotto II 259 No. 186; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: Parson tells borrower to get the corn at the same place as he got it last year. There is none there. " Then you didn't return it as you said you would, and there is none to lend you this year.

name: Payers of cash favored.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The one to blow the whistle. Many persons request a man on the way to market to buy them a whistle. Finally one hands him a coin with the request. "You are the one who shall blow the whistle."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Many persons request a man on the way to market to buy them a whistle. Finally one hands him a coin with the request. " You are the one who shall blow the whistle.

name: The weighted order-cards. To a man going on a voyage, various people give commissions for purchases, which he writes down on cards. Some give him money; some promise it on his return. On ship he looks the cards over, placing the proper gold on the proper card. A wind blows into the sea those not weighted with gold.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: To a man going on a voyage, various people give commissions for purchases, which he writes down on cards. Some give him money; some promise it on his return. On ship he looks the cards over, placing the proper gold on the proper card. A wind blows into the sea those not weighted with gold.

name: Unstable security. Stag tries to borrow grain from the sheep, using the wolf as security. Sheep says that they are both so swift that he does not know where they will be on the day of payment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Stag tries to borrow grain from the sheep, using the wolf as security. Sheep says that they are both so swift that he does not know where they will be on the day of payment.

name: A three thousand year old debt. Guests in inn discuss reincarnation. "Since we shall come back in three thousand years, the host might trust us till then." Host: "You still owe me what you didn't pay three thousand years ago."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Guests in inn discuss reincarnation. " Since we shall come back in three thousand years, the host might trust us till then. " Host: "You still owe me what you didn't pay three thousand years ago.

name: Retorts concerning thefts.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Thief makes a lame excuse.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: INDIA . Stokes 214 .; Indonesian: Coster-Wijsman Uilspiegel Verhalen 35ff. Nos. 17–22.

name: Thief's excuse: the big wind. Vegetable thief is caught in a garden. Owner: How did you get into the garden? A wind blew me in. How were the vegetables uprooted? If the wind is strong enough to blow me in, it can uproot them. How did they get into your bag? That is what I was just wondering.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 207 No. 7, cf. II 214 No. 441.
additional_description: Vegetable thief is caught in a garden. Owner: How did you get into the garden? A wind blew me in. How were the vegetables uprooted? If the wind is strong enough to blow me in, it can uproot them. How did they get into your bag? That is what I was just wondering.

name: The ladder market. A thief climbs over a wall by means of his ladder. When caught in the garden he says that he is a seller of ladders. Owner: "Is this a ladder market?" "Can't one sell ladders everywhere?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A thief climbs over a wall by means of his ladder. When caught in the garden he says that he is a seller of ladders. Owner: "Is this a ladder market? " "Can't one sell ladders everywhere?

name: The sheep's teeth. Two thieves caught with stolen sheep. One says that he told the other than sheep have lower teeth but no upper, and that they caught the sheep to see.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: NILGIRI . Kota : Emeneau .; Africa (Vai): Ellis 221 No. 34.
additional_description: Two thieves caught with stolen sheep. One says that he told the other than sheep have lower teeth but no upper, and that they caught the sheep to see.

name: Bitten by the sheep. Man caught just as he knocks sheep in the head: The sheep bit me (or: I'm not going to let sheep butt me to death).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man caught just as he knocks sheep in the head: The sheep bit me (or: I'm not going to let sheep butt me to death).

name: How he would act if he were a hawk. A hawk steals a piece of liver from a trickster and flies away with it. The trickster likewise steals some liver from another man and escapes to a high place with it. He says that he is seeing how he would act if he were a hawk.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A hawk steals a piece of liver from a trickster and flies away with it. The trickster likewise steals some liver from another man and escapes to a high place with it. He says that he is seeing how he would act if he were a hawk.

name: Fox pretends that he has been to the farmyard only to see if any of his kindred were there.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Lame excuse: one cannot drink because he has no teeth.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Were merely measuring cup. King demands that each subject bring small amount of milk to put in his new cup. They plan to cheat him by bringing him water. Accused, they say that they were merely measuring the cup to see how much it would take to fill it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: King demands that each subject bring small amount of milk to put in his new cup. They plan to cheat him by bringing him water. Accused, they say that they were merely measuring the cup to see how much it would take to fill it.

name: Needles and anchors. Fox leaving merchant's warehouse: "I had wanted a needle as big as an anchor and an anchor as small as a needle."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Fox leaving merchant's warehouse: "I had wanted a needle as big as an anchor and an anchor as small as a needle.

name: Owner assists thief.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Thief followed home. A thief ransacks a man's house and departs. The man gathers together the rest of his belongings and tracks the thief. Comes to the thief's door. Thief: "What do you want with me?" "What, isn't this the house we set out for?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A thief ransacks a man's house and departs. The man gathers together the rest of his belongings and tracks the thief. Comes to the thief's door. Thief: "What do you want with me? " "What, isn't this the house we set out for?

name: Robbers commiserated. A buffoon says to robbers in his house, "You can't find anything here in the dark, for I can find nothing in broad daylight."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Bebel I 132 No. 32; Scala Celi 104b No. 567; Mensa Philosophica No. 62.
additional_description: A buffoon says to robbers in his house, "You can't find anything here in the dark, for I can find nothing in broad daylight.

name: Owner advises thieves to return later. He is not yet in bed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: He is not yet in bed Spanish: Childers.

name: Cynic helps robber steal his money so he and robber can get sleep.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Owner laughs at thief who finds nothing in house.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Swynnerton Raja Rasalu 197 .

name: Bridegroom promises thief spoils later if he will not interfere with wedding.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The double fool. A numskull caught changing meal from others' sacks into his own. Miller asks him what he is doing. "I am a fool." "Why then don't you put your meal into their sacks?" "I am only a simple fool. If I did that I should be a double fool."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A numskull caught changing meal from others' sacks into his own. Miller asks him what he is doing. " I am a fool. " "Why then don't you put your meal into their sacks? " "I am only a simple fool. If I did that I should be a double fool.

name: Thieves' nocturnal habits.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Night study. A thief having scorned Demosthenes for his constant study, the latter says, "I know that you have not failed to notice that I study much at night."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A thief having scorned Demosthenes for his constant study, the latter says, "I know that you have not failed to notice that I study much at night.

name: Man who rises too early. The king in order to correct the habit has him robbed. He says that robbers get up even earlier than he.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The king in order to correct the habit has him robbed. He says that robbers get up even earlier than he.

name: Was going to give it to him any way. Thus a hunter answers a thief who steals his hare.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 84 (ET 504), 125 (ST 337); Halm Aesop No. 163.
additional_description: Thus a hunter answers a thief who steals his hare.

name: Removing chance for worry. A king noticing that a student has stolen a capon, asks, "Does not the Bible say that you should not worry about tomorrow?" "Exactly. I was trying to remove all chance of worry tomorrow."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wesselski Mönchslatein 109 No. 92; Nouvelles Récréations No. 35.
additional_description: A king noticing that a student has stolen a capon, asks, "Does not the Bible say that you should not worry about tomorrow? " "Exactly. I was trying to remove all chance of worry tomorrow.

name: The cost price recovered. A man takes a shirt to market for a friend who has stolen it. At market it is stolen from the seller. He tells his friend that the market was bad and that he was able to get back only the cost price (nothing).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man takes a shirt to market for a friend who has stolen it. At market it is stolen from the seller. He tells his friend that the market was bad and that he was able to get back only the cost price (nothing).

name: Compliments from the hangman. A man complimented a hangman on the good job he had done in hanging a thief. The hangman takes off his hat: "One thief I hang, to the other I take off my hat."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man complimented a hangman on the good job he had done in hanging a thief. The hangman takes off his hat: "One thief I hang, to the other I take off my hat.

name: Retorts concerning thefts – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The Lord has Risen. A parson hides his money in a holy place and leaves a sign, "The Lord is in this Place." A thief takes the money and leaves a sign, "He is risen and is no longer here"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A parson hides his money in a holy place and leaves a sign, "The Lord is in this Place. " A thief takes the money and leaves a sign, "He is risen and is no longer here" Pauli (ed.

name: Repartee concerning false reform.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The tailor's dream. A tailor dreams that at Judgment Day he sees a flag made up of all the pieces of cloth he has stolen Upon waking he asks his servants to warn him if they ever see him tempted to steal again. This happens. He replies, "The piece I am about to steal does not fit into the flag."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A tailor dreams that at Judgment Day he sees a flag made up of all the pieces of cloth he has stolen Upon waking he asks his servants to warn him if they ever see him tempted to steal again. This happens. He replies, "The piece I am about to steal does not fit into the flag.

name: Repartee concerning fatness.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The hay wagon and the gate. A parson arriving late at a city gate asks if he can get in. Guard sees that he is fat and in fun says that he doesn't know. The parson: "Why not; doesn't the hay wagon get in?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A parson arriving late at a city gate asks if he can get in. Guard sees that he is fat and in fun says that he doesn't know. The parson: "Why not; doesn't the hay wagon get in?

name: Animals retort concerning their dangers.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1420. Animals retort concerning their dangers.

name: Peace among the animals. (Peace fable.) The fox tries to beguile the cock by reporting a new law establishing peace among the animals. Dogs appear; the fox flees. "The dogs have not heard of the new law."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1420. Animals retort concerning their dangers.
bibliographies: *Type 62; *BP II 207; Wienert FFC LVI 52 (ET 120), 98 (ST 125); Halm Aesop No. 225; Jacobs Aesop 214 No. 59; *Chauvin II 202 No. 51, V 241 No. 141; *Lancaster PMLA XXII 33; *Graf FFC XXXVIII 26; Fb "ræv" III 114a. Spanish: Espinosa III No. 225; India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Swynnerton Raja Rasalu 184. - BOMBAY . Marathi : Dexter 171 No. 48
additional_description: (Peace fable.) The fox tries to beguile the cock by reporting a new law establishing peace among the animals. Dogs appear; the fox flees. " The dogs have not heard of the new law.

name: Good bath. A cat seeing a mouse leave a bath says, "Good bath!" Mouse: "If I had not seen you!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1420. Animals retort concerning their dangers.
additional_description: A cat seeing a mouse leave a bath says, "Good bath! " Mouse: "If I had not seen you!

name: Roast falcon. A falcon reproaches a cock for fleeing from the master who has fed him. The cock: "I have never seen a falcon roasted."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1420. Animals retort concerning their dangers.
additional_description: A falcon reproaches a cock for fleeing from the master who has fed him. The cock: "I have never seen a falcon roasted.

name: Where the foxes will meet. Two foxes in a trap converse: "Where shall we meet again?" "In three days at the furrier's."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1420. Animals retort concerning their dangers.
additional_description: Two foxes in a trap converse: "Where shall we meet again? " "In three days at the furrier's.

name: Repartee concerning doctors and patients.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1430. Repartee concerning doctors and patients.

name: I know not how. Sick man: "I came to a place I know not where; something happened I know not how; I am sick I know not where." Doctor: "Go to the pharmacy and buy I know not what, and eat it I know not how, and you will become well I know not when."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1430. Repartee concerning doctors and patients.
additional_description: Sick man: "I came to a place I know not where; something happened I know not how; I am sick I know not where. " Doctor: "Go to the pharmacy and buy I know not what, and eat it I know not how, and you will become well I know not when.

name: No physician at all. A bad physician having predicted the immediate death of a patient meets him recovered. "How go things down below?" "They put you at the head of the list of bad physicians, but I maintained that you were no physician at all."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1430. Repartee concerning doctors and patients.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 39; Halm Aesop No. 168.
additional_description: A bad physician having predicted the immediate death of a patient meets him recovered. " How go things down below? " "They put you at the head of the list of bad physicians, but I maintained that you were no physician at all.

name: "Do as I say and not as I do." Doctor forbids patient to drink wine. When patient reproves doctor for drinking wine the latter says: "Just now it is bad for you and good for me."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1430. Repartee concerning doctors and patients.
additional_description: "  Doctor forbids patient to drink wine. When patient reproves doctor for drinking wine the latter says: "Just now it is bad for you and good for me.

name: Strenuous cure for madness. Doctor throws patients into a pit of water. Servant warns queer-looking hunter to flee before master throws him into the pit. (Cf. K2137.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1430. Repartee concerning doctors and patients.
additional_description: Doctor throws patients into a pit of water. Servant warns queer-looking hunter to flee before master throws him into the pit.

name: Veterinarian becomes doctor. When he killed animals he had to pay for them; but he did not have to pay for killing people.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1430. Repartee concerning doctors and patients.
additional_description: When he killed animals he had to pay for them; but he did not have to pay for killing people.

name: Cannot see goods . Physician curing blindness carries away woman's goods . She refuses payment , saying she cannot see any goods in her house.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: SIND . Grierson Survey VIII ( 1 ) 211. _ CENT . INDIA . Grierson Survey IX ( 2 ) 125 .

name: Repartee – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: God of the earth. Question from the king: "Who are you?" "I am God." "Make my eyes larger." "I am only God of the earth and have power only below the girdle."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Question from the king: "Who are you? " "I am God. " "Make my eyes larger. " "I am only God of the earth and have power only below the girdle.

name: A cynic's retorts.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The cynic wants sunlight. King (to cynic): "What can I do for you?" "Get out of my sunlight. Don't take away from me what you can't give me."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 802; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: King (to cynic): "What can I do for you? " "Get out of my sunlight. Don't take away from me what you can't give me.

name: Cynic praises power of reason. Tells Alexander that the power of reason makes even a poor man as great as a king.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Tells Alexander that the power of reason makes even a poor man as great as a king.

name: The cynic at the bath. Leaving an unclean bath house: "Where can I go now to wash?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Leaving an unclean bath house: "Where can I go now to wash?

name: The cynic and the pale gold. "Why is gold so pale?" "It is in great danger."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Why is gold so pale? " "It is in great danger.

name: The cynic's burial. Asked who will carry him to his grave if he has no friends: "He who needs my house."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Asked who will carry him to his grave if he has no friends: "He who needs my house.

name: Cynic asks that his body be exposed to the elements. When told that the beasts and the birds will prey on him he says: "Put a stick at my side so that I may chase them away."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: When told that the beasts and the birds will prey on him he says: "Put a stick at my side so that I may chase them away.

name: The cynic as judge of wine. Asked which wine tastes best, he says, "That belonging to other people."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Asked which wine tastes best, he says, "That belonging to other people.

name: The cynic and the big gates. Coming to a little town with big gates, he says, "Close the gate so that the town won't run away."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Coming to a little town with big gates, he says, "Close the gate so that the town won't run away.

name: The cynic and the bastard stone-thrower. Cynic: "Be careful; you might hit your father."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 802; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: Cynic: "Be careful; you might hit your father.

name: The cynic discusses heaven. Hearing a man discoursing at great length about heaven, he asks, "When did you come down from there?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Hearing a man discoursing at great length about heaven, he asks, "When did you come down from there?

name: The cynic and the bald-headed man. His only reply to the baldheaded man's slanders is to compliment the hair that has left such a horrible head.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: His only reply to the baldheaded man's slanders is to compliment the hair that has left such a horrible head.

name: The cynic and the deceiver. When the deceiver calls him wicked, he says, "I am glad that you are my enemy; for you do good to your enemies and evil to your friends."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: When the deceiver calls him wicked, he says, "I am glad that you are my enemy; for you do good to your enemies and evil to your friends.

name: The cynic and the fig tree. Man tells friend that his wife has hanged herself on a fig tree. Friend: "Give me a shoot of that tree!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man tells friend that his wife has hanged herself on a fig tree. Friend: "Give me a shoot of that tree!

name: The cynic's wish. When he learns that a woman has hanged herself from a tree he explains: "Would that all trees bore such fruit!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: When he learns that a woman has hanged herself from a tree he explains: "Would that all trees bore such fruit!

name: Cynic is asked if widower should remarry. "One who has just escaped from drowning should not return to sea."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: One who has just escaped from drowning should not return to sea.

name: The smallest woman makes the best bride. "Of an evil choose the smallest part."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Of an evil choose the smallest part.

name: The fools in the city. Man ordered to number the fools in the city replies, "It is easier to number the wise men."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 241 No. 535; Chauvin VII 126 No. 393bis.
additional_description: Man ordered to number the fools in the city replies, "It is easier to number the wise men.

name: They gave it away themselves. A wandering actor rewarded by a city with a coat of their color gambles it away. When upbraided about giving away their present he replies that they hadn't wanted to keep it themselves.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A wandering actor rewarded by a city with a coat of their color gambles it away. When upbraided about giving away their present he replies that they hadn't wanted to keep it themselves.

name: The forgotten traditions. A man has been told by a seer that there are two ways in which a believer may be distinguished. But he has forgotten one of them and the seer had forgotten the other.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man has been told by a seer that there are two ways in which a believer may be distinguished. But he has forgotten one of them and the seer had forgotten the other.

name: Aaron's censer. A man strikes a priest with a cane: "This is Moses' staff." The priest shoots with a pistol: "This is Aaron's holy censer."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man strikes a priest with a cane: "This is Moses' staff. " The priest shoots with a pistol: "This is Aaron's holy censer.

name: The favored swine. Dog reproaches sow that Venus will not allow those who have eaten swine to enter her temple. Sow says that it is because the goddess abhors those who kill swine.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 56 (ET 161), 107 (ST 191); Halm Aesop No. 408.
additional_description: Dog reproaches sow that Venus will not allow those who have eaten swine to enter her temple. Sow says that it is because the goddess abhors those who kill swine.

name: The contagious yawns. A husband planning to punish his wife, who has yawned in church at the same time as a man, sees his error when his wife in the woods calls out, "The squirrels hop from bough to bough as the yawns from mouth to mouth."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Finnish: Aarne FFC VIII 6 No. 23; XXXIII 52 No. 23; Estonian: Aarne FFC XXV 141 No. 12; Livonian: Loorits FFC LXVI 84 No. 35.
additional_description: A husband planning to punish his wife, who has yawned in church at the same time as a man, sees his error when his wife in the woods calls out, "The squirrels hop from bough to bough as the yawns from mouth to mouth.

name: Who gets the beehive. Badger: "I was a hundred years old when grama grass first grew." Crane: "My daughter was a hundred years old when grama grass first grew." Wolf: "I am only eight years old, but we shall see who gets the beehive."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Spanish: Boggs FFC XC 31 No. *80, Espinosa III Nos. 268–270, Espinosa Jr. Nos. 26, 27.
additional_description: Badger: "I was a hundred years old when grama grass first grew. " Crane: "My daughter was a hundred years old when grama grass first grew. " Wolf: "I am only eight years old, but we shall see who gets the beehive.

name: Why he was thin. Philosopher explains that with his own blood he was nourishing as large a population as that of the Roman Emperor (lice).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Philosopher explains that with his own blood he was nourishing as large a population as that of the Roman Emperor (lice).

name: It's better to fight in the shade. Soldier tells captain that the enemy are so numerous that their arrows darken the sun. Captain: "Good, it will be more comfortable fighting in the shade."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Soldier tells captain that the enemy are so numerous that their arrows darken the sun. Captain: "Good, it will be more comfortable fighting in the shade.

name: The lion and the statue. A man points out the statue to show the supremacy of man. The lion: "If it had been a lion sculptor, the lion would have been standing over the man."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 70 (ET 343), 101 (ST 145); Halm Aesop No. 63; Jacobs Aesop 208 No. 35.
additional_description: A man points out the statue to show the supremacy of man. The lion: "If it had been a lion sculptor, the lion would have been standing over the man.

name: Has never died yet. Slave (workman) recommended to master whose recent slaves have died: "He has never died while I owned him."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Slave (workman) recommended to master whose recent slaves have died: "He has never died while I owned him.

name: The liar. A man attempts to lie out of having called another a liar: "You lie if you say that I said you lied." The other: "It's a good thing for you that you didn't call me a liar." (Cf. J817.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man attempts to lie out of having called another a liar: "You lie if you say that I said you lied. " The other: "It's a good thing for you that you didn't call me a liar.

name: The gray fox. An old husband tells his young wife, who is concerned about his gray hair, "A gray fox is as good as a red one." "But an old gray fox is not so good as a young red one."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: An old husband tells his young wife, who is concerned about his gray hair, "A gray fox is as good as a red one. " "But an old gray fox is not so good as a young red one.

name: An oath to break oaths. Village called on to join in war deliberates in meeting. A man says, "We have taken oaths not to go to war. We must now take an oath to break all the oaths we have taken."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Village called on to join in war deliberates in meeting. A man says, "We have taken oaths not to go to war. We must now take an oath to break all the oaths we have taken.

name: The cause of grayness. Fool asked what made him gray-headed replies, "My hair."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Fool asked what made him gray-headed replies, "My hair.

name: Customary suits. Man who wears a red cap after his father's death says, "I can grieve just as much in red as in black."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man who wears a red cap after his father's death says, "I can grieve just as much in red as in black.

name: A long beard and sanctity. Told that a forest dwarf with a long beard is a saint, a man replies: "If a long beard indicates sanctity, the goat is a saint."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Told that a forest dwarf with a long beard is a saint, a man replies: "If a long beard indicates sanctity, the goat is a saint.

name: Trumpeter's false defense. A trumpeter captured pleads that he did not fight. Answered: "You may not fight, but you encourage your men to do so."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A trumpeter captured pleads that he did not fight. Answered: "You may not fight, but you encourage your men to do so.

name: Must drink from the common cup. A man always drinks out of his own cup. In storm at sea a sailor says, "Today we all drink out of the same cup (the sea).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man always drinks out of his own cup. In storm at sea a sailor says, "Today we all drink out of the same cup (the sea).

name: Sailor prays for gods to sink ship during storm, since gods never do what they are asked to do.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Not in good form. A duke invites a notorious eater. The latter consumes eight fowls, forty eggs, and other things in proportion. In leaving he apologizes for eating so little as he had not felt well the night before. He will do better next time.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A duke invites a notorious eater. The latter consumes eight fowls, forty eggs, and other things in proportion. In leaving he apologizes for eating so little as he had not felt well the night before. He will do better next time.

name: The eunuch's defense. Reproached with his mutilation, the eunuch says that his ill fortune is no shame. The only shame is to merit what you suffer.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Reproached with his mutilation, the eunuch says that his ill fortune is no shame. The only shame is to merit what you suffer.

name: The fairest thing in the garden. Three brothers asked by princess what is the most beautiful thing in the garden. The youngest replies, "Yourself." He wins the princess.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Three brothers asked by princess what is the most beautiful thing in the garden. The youngest replies, "Yourself." He wins the princess.

name: The greedy dreamer. He dreams that he receives nine coins. He demands ten. He wakes and finds that he has dreamed. He is willing to accept the nine.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 206 (No. 5); Spanish Exempla: Keller.
additional_description: He dreams that he receives nine coins. He demands ten. He wakes and finds that he has dreamed. He is willing to accept the nine.

name: The 999 gold pieces. A man prays for a thousand gold pieces and says that he will not accept one less. A joker sends him 999. He says that he will trust God for the other coin.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man prays for a thousand gold pieces and says that he will not accept one less. A joker sends him 999. He says that he will trust God for the other coin.

name: The fatal bed. A sailor says that all his ancestors have drowned. Citizen: "Aren't you afraid of drowning?" Sailor: "How did your ancestors die?" Citizen: "All of them in bed." Sailor: "Aren't you afraid to go to bed?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A sailor says that all his ancestors have drowned. Citizen: "Aren't you afraid of drowning?" Sailor: "How did your ancestors die?" Citizen: "All of them in bed." Sailor: "Aren't you afraid to go to bed?"

name: Is ready to go. A peddler scolds the dog who is waiting and tells him to get ready to go with him. The dog replies that he has nothing to carry, that it is the peddler who is late.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A peddler scolds the dog who is waiting and tells him to get ready to go with him. The dog replies that he has nothing to carry, that it is the peddler who is late.

name: The proud hide humbled. A hide is in the river. River: "What is your name?" Hide: "Hard hide." River: "Hunt another name; I'll soon soften you."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 75 (ET 403), 101 (ST 148); Halm Aesop No. 381.
additional_description: A hide is in the river. River: "What is your name?" Hide: "Hard hide." River: "Hunt another name; I'll soon soften you."

name: Demonstrate here. A man boasts of his jump on Rhodes and says that if he were in Rhodes he could prove his boast. Reply: "No need to go to Rhodes. Show us your jump here."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 82 (ET 480), 100 (ST 135); Halm Aesop No. 203.
additional_description: A man boasts of his jump on Rhodes and says that if he were in Rhodes he could prove his boast. Reply: "No need to go to Rhodes. Show us your jump here."

name: Husband and wife burn their mouths. A wife served overhot soup, forgets and burns herself so that tears come to her eyes She says that her departed father liked soup so much that she weeps when she eats it. The husband is also burned and weeps: "I am weeping because your accursed mother didn't take you with her when she died."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 238 No. 115; Köhler-Bolte I 498; Indonesian: Coster-Wijsman 71 No. 129.
additional_description: A wife served overhot soup, forgets and burns herself so that tears come to her eyes She says that her departed father liked soup so much that she weeps when she eats it. The husband is also burned and weeps: "I am weeping because your accursed mother didn't take you with her when she died."

name: The abbot burns his mouth. Says that he is crying because so many have left the monastery.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Says that he is crying because so many have left the monastery.

name: As you surely will. After her husband's death a woman cannot find a hammer and anvil. She goes to a dying neighbor and says, "If you die, as you surely will, and go to Heaven, as you surely will not, ask my husband where he left the hammer and anvil." The dying man's wife replies, "If you go to Heaven, as you surely will, if you die as you surely will not, do not run around and get into trouble, but sit down by the Eternal Father and observe and keep still."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: After her husband's death a woman cannot find a hammer and anvil. She goes to a dying neighbor and says, "If you die, as you surely will, and go to Heaven, as you surely will not, ask my husband where he left the hammer and anvil." The dying man's wife replies, "If you go to Heaven, as you surely will, if you die as you surely will not, do not run around and get into trouble, but sit down by the Eternal Father and observe and keep still."

name: Keeping the secret. Man tells parson secret and asks him to keep it. The parson refuses; "If you can't keep the secret, you must not expect me to."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man tells parson secret and asks him to keep it. The parson refuses; "If you can't keep the secret, you must not expect me to."

name: Repartee concerning runaway horses.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: King and jester flee: the king's swift horse. Jester: "You did have a swift horse. When I had to stop because my horse was tired out, you went two miles further."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Jester: "You did have a swift horse. When I had to stop because my horse was tired out, you went two miles further."

name: Where his mule will. A man on a runaway mule is asked, "Where are you going?" "Wherever my mule wants to."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin II 181 No. 343; Italian Novella: Rotunda; Japanese: Ikeda.
additional_description: A man on a runaway mule is asked, "Where are you going?" "Wherever my mule wants to."

name: Storms on land. An inexperienced rider borrows a horse, which runs away with him. He says, "There are no such storms on sea as on the land."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: An inexperienced rider borrows a horse, which runs away with him. He says, "There are no such storms on sea as on the land."

name: "Why didn't you stay on the horse?" The rider: "I couldn't; you see, it ended there" (pointing to horse's head).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: KASHMIR . Jammu : Brown Tawi MS No. 54 .
additional_description: The rider: "I couldn't; you see, it ended there" (pointing to horse's head).

name: The sound of shaving. An unskillful barber keeps cutting a man's face. A noise is heard. The man: "What is that?" Barber: "A smith shoeing horses." Man: "I thought it was someone being shaved."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: An unskillful barber keeps cutting a man's face. A noise is heard. The man: "What is that?" Barber: "A smith shoeing horses." Man: "I thought it was someone being shaved."

name: Mistaken identity. A bride slips up behind her husband and kisses him. He is annoyed. She: "Pardon me, I did not know it was you"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A bride slips up behind her husband and kisses him. He is annoyed. She: "Pardon me, I did not know it was you" Clouston Noodles 94.

name: Husband mistaken for lover in bed. Farmer has whiskers shaved off, hair cut short. He gets home late, slips into bed with his wife. She runs her hand over his face, says: "Young man, if you're goin' to do anything, you'd better be agittin' at it, 'cause Old Whiskers'll be here pretty soon."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Farmer has whiskers shaved off, hair cut short. He gets home late, slips into bed with his wife. She runs her hand over his face, says: "Young man, if you're goin' to do anything, you'd better be agittin' at it, 'cause Old Whiskers'll be here pretty soon."

name: Listening to the debate. A lazy youth explains his late rising by saying that he lay abed to hear the argument between industry and laziness.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A lazy youth explains his late rising by saying that he lay abed to hear the argument between industry and laziness.

name: Progress in school. A man sends his two sons to schools and later enquires of their progress. One: "I am past Grace." The other: "I am at the devil and all his works."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man sends his two sons to schools and later enquires of their progress. One: "I am past Grace." The other: "I am at the devil and all his works."
