name: What the bear whispered in his ear. Paid guide climbs tree and leaves traveler to mercy of a bear. Traveler feigns death and the bear sniffs at him and leaves. The guide: "What did the bear say to you?" "He said, never trust a coward like you."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wienert FFC LVI 68 (ET 319), 126 (ST 349); Halm Aesop No. 311; Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 422; India: Thompson-Balys: BOMBAY . Grierson Survey VII 137
additional_description: Paid guide climbs tree and leaves traveler to mercy of a bear. Traveler feigns death and the bear sniffs at him and leaves. The guide: "What did the bear say to you?" "He said, never trust a coward like you."

name: Artist paints too few birds. Is engaged to decorate room. Owner: "You did not paint as many birds as I told you to." Artist: "The windows were left open and most of them flew out."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Is engaged to decorate room. Owner: "You did not paint as many birds as I told you to." Artist: "The windows were left open and most of them flew out."

name: Trickster artist hoodwinks king: cuckold's eyes cannot see picture. King pretends to see the picture. Courtiers reveal that there is no picture.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: King pretends to see the picture. Courtiers reveal that there is no picture.

name: Daydreamer has lost his chance for profit. He has broken his master's pots while dreaming of future profits. He has therefore lost more than the master and excuses himself of blame.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Santal : Bompas 141 No. 30 ; Bodding II 77ff . No. 34
additional_description: He has broken his master's pots while dreaming of future profits. He has therefore lost more than the master and excuses himself of blame.

name: Why captain takes lame and one-eyed soldiers into army. The lame cannot flee from the enemy; the one-eyed soldiers will not see enough to make them afraid.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The lame cannot flee from the enemy; the one-eyed soldiers will not see enough to make them afraid.

name: Person runs from actual or supposed ghost. (Cf. E293, J1483.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Man runs from actual or from supposed ghost. The ghost runs beside him. The man stops to rest; the ghost stops, says, "That was a good run we had!" The man says, "Yes, and as soon as I get my breath, I'm going to run some more."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The ghost runs beside him. The man stops to rest; the ghost stops, says, "That was a good run we had!" The man says, "Yes, and as soon as I get my breath, I'm going to run some more."

name: When Caleb comes. Man attempts to stay in haunted house all night. One cat after another enters, says, "Everything will be all right when Caleb comes, won't it?" (or something similar). Man finally says, "When Caleb comes, tell him I was here and left." He leaves.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man attempts to stay in haunted house all night. One cat after another enters, says, "Everything will be all right when Caleb comes, won't it?" (or something similar). Man finally says, "When Caleb comes, tell him I was here and left." He leaves.

name: Clever practical retort.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The cheater cheated.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: A rule must work both ways.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Make-believe eating, make-believe work. At table the peasant says, "We will only act as if we were eating." At work the servant replies, "We will only act as if we were working."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: At table the peasant says, "We will only act as if we were eating." At work the servant replies, "We will only act as if we were working."

name: Turnips called bacon: cat called rabbit. A peasant compels his servant to call turnips bacon. Under favorable circumstances the servant compels the master to call a cat a rabbit.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A peasant compels his servant to call turnips bacon. Under favorable circumstances the servant compels the master to call a cat a rabbit.

name: Man bathing rich man uses sand instead of sandalwood: "Consider the sand of the Ganges to be sandalwood." When man asks for his fee, other takes a frog and gives it to him: "Know that the frog of the Ganges is a cow."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: When man asks for his fee, other takes a frog and gives it to him: "Know that the frog of the Ganges is a cow."

name: Eating cure becomes epidemic. A woman feigns periodic attacks of a sickness that can be cured only by eating a great number of delicacies. The husband feigns the same disease.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A woman feigns periodic attacks of a sickness that can be cured only by eating a great number of delicacies. The husband feigns the same disease.

name: Fits become epidemic. After dinner a servant feigns a fit and goes to sleep. The master thereupon feigns a fit and beats the boy, who is thus cured of his laziness.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: After dinner a servant feigns a fit and goes to sleep. The master thereupon feigns a fit and beats the boy, who is thus cured of his laziness.

name: The wine-spilling host rebuked. A host spills his customer's wine so that he must buy more. He consoles the guest with "It is a sign of the great abundance you shall have this year." With the same remark the guest draws the spigot from the host's wine cask.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 372; *Wesselski Mönchslatein No. 106; *Crane Vitry 269 No. 310.
additional_description: A host spills his customer's wine so that he must buy more. He consoles the guest with "It is a sign of the great abundance you shall have this year." With the same remark the guest draws the spigot from the host's wine cask.

name: The porter's revenge for the three wise counsels. A man offers a porter three wise counsels for carrying his goods. "When anyone tells you that hunger and satisfaction are the same, don't believe him." The second and third are similar and equally valueless. The porter throws down the load: "When anyone tells you that any part of this load is not broken, don't believe him."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Basset 1001 Contes II 391; Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 260 No. 211; Chauvin VIII 139 No. 136; *Fb "sandhed".
additional_description: A man offers a porter three wise counsels for carrying his goods. "When anyone tells you that hunger and satisfaction are the same, don't believe him." The second and third are similar and equally valueless. The porter throws down the load: "When anyone tells you that any part of this load is not broken, don't believe him."

name: No clothes needed for Day of Judgment. Friends tell a man that the next day is the Day of Judgment and urge him to kill a lamb and give a feast. He apparently consents. He then burns up their clothes. They will not need clothes on the Day of Judgment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Friends tell a man that the next day is the Day of Judgment and urge him to kill a lamb and give a feast. He apparently consents. He then burns up their clothes. They will not need clothes on the Day of Judgment.

name: Forbidden to think. A husband forbids a wife to think. During his absence she roasts a chicken, eats it, and leaves two drumsticks on the table. The husband asks for the chicken. "Since you have forbidden me to think, I did not think of you." The ban is lifted.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A husband forbids a wife to think. During his absence she roasts a chicken, eats it, and leaves two drumsticks on the table. The husband asks for the chicken. "Since you have forbidden me to think, I did not think of you." The ban is lifted.

name: Master says that he has eyes in back of head: servant cheats him. Holds up food to master's back and then not having objection raised, eats it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: ASSAM HILLS . Kachari : Anderson 34 ; Endle 63f . No. 3 .
additional_description: Holds up food to master's back and then not having objection raised, eats it.

name: Counterfeit money burned up. A priest who has lent money to a Jew, but will not lend to a farmer, on being reproached by the latter, says that the money he lent the Jew was "false". When the Jew gets to know of this, he claims that as soon as he heard the money was false he burnt it up.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A priest who has lent money to a Jew, but will not lend to a farmer, on being reproached by the latter, says that the money he lent the Jew was "false". When the Jew gets to know of this, he claims that as soon as he heard the money was false he burnt it up.

name: Boy pretends to speak only Latin. Father, chastising his lazy son, also speaks in "Latin".
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Lithuanian: Balys Index No. *2423; Russian: Andrejev No. *2082.
additional_description: Father, chastising his lazy son, also speaks in "Latin".

name: The man in place of a watch-dog. The master orders his serf to watch the manor at night in place of the dog. When the thieves come, the serf barks: "Dress, dress .... They take, take .... They lead, lead .... " The master does not understand the barking and pays no heed to it – is robbed of his property.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The master orders his serf to watch the manor at night in place of the dog. When the thieves come, the serf barks: "Dress, dress .... They take, take .... They lead, lead .... " The master does not understand the barking and pays no heed to it   is robbed of his property.

name: Oisin's poor diet in Patrick's house – pancake size of ivy leaf, measure of butter only size of rowan berry. Later Oisin gives Patrick quarter of a wild boar, servant ivy leaf and rowan berry.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Later Oisin gives Patrick quarter of a wild boar, servant ivy leaf and rowan berry.

name: Things on highway belong to the public. Man reproves another for picking his cherries that overhang the highway. He is told that things on the public highway belong to no one in particular. He invites the man to climb the tree for better cherries, drives off with his horse and buggy, giving the same answer about things in the public highway.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man reproves another for picking his cherries that overhang the highway. He is told that things on the public highway belong to no one in particular. He invites the man to climb the tree for better cherries, drives off with his horse and buggy, giving the same answer about things in the public highway.

name: Can drink only one kind of wine at a time. Priests come to an inn where host gives them good wine and bad. They insist on having all good since they must drink only one kind of wine. On their return he serves them with all bad. They claim to have had the rule changed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Priests come to an inn where host gives them good wine and bad. They insist on having all good since they must drink only one kind of wine. On their return he serves them with all bad. They claim to have had the rule changed.

name: "Eat spiritual food, not material," say monks to lazy brother who criticizes them for working in the garden.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Ox bought; buyer also claims load of wood attached. Later deceived man disguises and sells sharper another ox for "handful of coppers." He is allowed by court to claim the hand as well.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Later deceived man disguises and sells sharper another ox for "handful of coppers." He is allowed by court to claim the hand as well.

name: Priests say dying woman's unfulfilled request for mangoes must be paid in golden mangoes to them. Priests burned with iron since she had asked to be cauterized.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MADRAS . Natesa Sastri Tennalirama 15f .
additional_description: Priests burned with iron since she had asked to be cauterized.

name: Wife surrendered to king. Vizier advises doctor to give up his wife to love-sick prince since everything belongs to a sovereign. Doctor then tells him prince is in love not with his (the doctor's) wife but with the vizier's.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Vizier advises doctor to give up his wife to love-sick prince since everything belongs to a sovereign. Doctor then tells him prince is in love not with his (the doctor's) wife but with the vizier's. India: Thompson-Balys.

name: Goldsmith sells thinly plated gold; peasant retaliates: a pot of dirt with a little gyav on top. (Cf. J1556.1.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Impossible demand rebuked.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Milk from the hornless cow. A king demands a hundred men's drink from the milk of a hornless dun cow from every house in the land. Wooden cows are made and bog-stuff substituted for milk; the king must drink it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A king demands a hundred men's drink from the milk of a hornless dun cow from every house in the land. Wooden cows are made and bog-stuff substituted for milk; the king must drink it.

name: To return the eye to the one-eyed man. "Let me have your other so that I can see whether the one I bring you matches."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: BENGAL . Devi , S. 132. ASSAM VALLEY Lewison JRAS Beng . V 387. CENT . INDIA . Bastar : Elwin ( 1944 ) 488 .
additional_description: "Let me have your other so that I can see whether the one I bring you matches."

name: Healed with his own medicine.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: "If he does not live, let him die." The student as healer hangs this sign around the neck of a sick calf. Later as parson he is sick. It is proposed to heal him with the same remedy.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The student as healer hangs this sign around the neck of a sick calf. Later as parson he is sick. It is proposed to heal him with the same remedy.

name: You lead and I will follow: hard command thus evaded.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The father of an illegitimate child must walk in front of the cross. Condemned man insists that the priest, who is guilty also, shall lead the way.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Condemned man insists that the priest, who is guilty also, shall lead the way.

name: Rogues exchange objects and cheat each other.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: MADRAS . Natesa Sastri Folk- Tales 494 ; Telugu : Pantulu Telugus 63f .

name: Thieves deceived by prearranged conversation which they overhear. (Cf. K420.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Overheard conversation: "Mustard has advanced in price." Thieves steal mustard and leave all else.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Thieves steal mustard and leave all else.

name: Overheard conversation: "Money hidden in wall (field)." Thieves take box filled with stone (or dig up field).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . Steel Punjab 222 ; Steel - Temple 234.- MADRAS . Natesa Sastri Tennalirama 23 .
additional_description: Thieves take box filled with stone (or dig up field).

name: Overheard conversation: "My money is hanging in the tree." Thieves stung by hornets.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Thieves stung by hornets.

name: Swindler's plans foiled.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: One eye ( leg ) to bring back another . Merchant told by blind man his father had taken his eye to sell . When man demands eye or money , merchant asks for re- maining eye to take with him so he can identify the other from among many . -BEN- GAL . Devi , S. 132.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: ASSAM VALLEY . Lewison JRAS Beng . V 387 .

name: The shoes carried into the tree. Tricksters induce a numskull to climb a tree, planning to steal his shoes. He takes them in his belt with him. "Perhaps I shall find a nearer road home up there and shall need my shoes."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Tricksters induce a numskull to climb a tree, planning to steal his shoes. He takes them in his belt with him. "Perhaps I shall find a nearer road home up there and shall need my shoes."

name: The old man nods "Yes". A monk at an old man's deathbed asks if he hasn't promised this and that to the church. The old man from weakness rather than understanding nods "Yes". The son standing by asks, "Shall I throw this fellow down stairs?" The old man nods "Yes".
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Bebel I 154 No. 81; Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 497.
additional_description: A monk at an old man's deathbed asks if he hasn't promised this and that to the church. The old man from weakness rather than understanding nods "Yes". The son standing by asks, "Shall I throw this fellow down stairs?" The old man nods "Yes".

name: Clever wife of king's adopted son keeps king distracted on deathbed so that he cannot disinherit husband.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Command would become permanent. A ruler receives gifts from his subjects and later demands them as he due. The fool sets the nobleman's bed on fire. When the nobleman commands him to put the fire out he refuses, since he would ever afterward have to be putting out fires.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A ruler receives gifts from his subjects and later demands them as he due. The fool sets the nobleman's bed on fire. When the nobleman commands him to put the fire out he refuses, since he would ever afterward have to be putting out fires.

name: Unjust oath countered by another. A woman entrusts three coins to a headsman. He denies her claim which she fails to substantiate in ordeal. She now claims more and happens to succeed with ordeal.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Santal : Bompas 46 No. 11
additional_description: A woman entrusts three coins to a headsman. He denies her claim which she fails to substantiate in ordeal. She now claims more and happens to succeed with ordeal.

name: Catching by words.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Bargain: to render service for "something". Claimant has called dead cricket "something" and must be content.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: GANGES VALLEY . Hindustani : Chilli 4lf
additional_description: Claimant has called dead cricket "something" and must be content.

name: Rebuke to the stingy.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Half price for half a shave. A man asks to be shaved at half price. The barber shaves one side. He must pay the other half for the rest of the shave.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man asks to be shaved at half price. The barber shaves one side. He must pay the other half for the rest of the shave.

name: Stingy man rebuked when his children are feasted in his absence. They think he has been responsible and honor him. He reforms.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Santal : Bodding II 10lff . No. 38 .
additional_description: They think he has been responsible and honor him. He reforms.

name: Poor girl outwits prince in fright-contest. He frightens her and later mocks her with her words of fright. She plays the same trick on him.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: He frightens her and later mocks her with her words of fright. She plays the same trick on him.

name: Soldier's practical retort to officer.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Officer disarmed by sentry. Army major approaches sentry, takes away his rifle, and reproaches him for allowing himself to be disarmed. Sentry draws pistol from inside shirt, demands return of unloaded rifle.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Army major approaches sentry, takes away his rifle, and reproaches him for allowing himself to be disarmed. Sentry draws pistol from inside shirt, demands return of unloaded rifle.

name: Dream answered by dream. Priests misinterpret raja's dream and get his horses. Trickster advises raja to announce dream demanding cauterization of the priests. They return horses.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Priests misinterpret raja's dream and get his horses. Trickster advises raja to announce dream demanding cauterization of the priests. They return horses.

name: One absurdity rebukes another.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Brown JAOS XXIX 43 n. 40, Penzer III 241, 250f., V 64ff., IX 152, 155; India: Thompson-Balys: BOMBAY . Salsette : D'Penha Ind . Antiq . XXIII 136ff .

name: Borrower's absurdities.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: England, Scotland, Ireland, Wales, U.S.: Baughman.

name: The transformed golden pumpkin. Borrower of golden pumpkin returns a brass pumpkin and claims that the gold has turned to brass. The lender takes the borrower's son and returns with an ape. He claims that the boy has turned into an ape.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Köhler-Bolte I 533; India: Thompson-Balys: INDO - TIBETAN . O'Connor No. 4
additional_description: Borrower of golden pumpkin returns a brass pumpkin and claims that the gold has turned to brass. The lender takes the borrower's son and returns with an ape. He claims that the boy has turned into an ape.

name: Mill has given birth to horse. Jackal as judge comes late. "Tank of water caught fire."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Jackal as judge comes late. "Tank of water caught fire."

name: The iron-eating mice. Trustee claims that mice have eaten the iron scales confided to him. The host abducts the trustee's son and says that a falcon has carried him off.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *BP II 372; Chauvin II 92 No. 37; Bødker Exempler; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: INDIA . Hertel 60 , 61. -KASHMIR . Knowles Proverbs 199f . - HIMALAYA . Upreti Proverbs 403. - CENT . INDIA . Maria : Rao , R. 16f . BOMBAY . Jethabhai 30-5 No. 10 ., Penzer III 250, V 62, *64; Indonesian: DeVries's list No. 299.
additional_description: Trustee claims that mice have eaten the iron scales confided to him. The host abducts the trustee's son and says that a falcon has carried him off.

name: The dog-eating bugs. Man keeps dog for boy, tells him when he comes for it that the chinch bugs have eaten it. The boy borrows a mule from the man, later tells him that a buzzard has carried it away. He gets his dog back.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man keeps dog for boy, tells him when he comes for it that the chinch bugs have eaten it. The boy borrows a mule from the man, later tells him that a buzzard has carried it away. He gets his dog back.

name: The pot has a child and dies. A borrower returns a pot along with a small one saying that the pot has had a young one. The pots are accepted. He borrows the pot a second time and keeps it. He sends word that the pot has died.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *BP II 372 n. 2; *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 213 No. 35; DeVries FFC LXXIII 273 n. 1; Rumanian: Schullerus FFC LXXVIII No. 1705*; India: Thompson-Balys: HIMALAYA . Upreti Proverbs 19f .
additional_description: A borrower returns a pot along with a small one saying that the pot has had a young one. The pots are accepted. He borrows the pot a second time and keeps it. He sends word that the pot has died.

name: Adulteress's absurdity rebuked.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The Snow-Child. (Modus Leibinc.) A sailor's wife bears a son in his absence and says that it came from eating snow. Later the husband makes away with the boy who, he says, melted in the sun.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Type 1362; *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 208; BP IV 130; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: (Modus Leibinc.) A sailor's wife bears a son in his absence and says that it came from eating snow. Later the husband makes away with the boy who, he says, melted in the sun.

name: Absurdities concerning birth of animals, or men. (Cf. J1531.3.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Absurdity : mill ( press ) has given birth to calf.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: [First Edition: J1533.1. Absurdity: mill has given birth to calf. – Oats planted on sea-shore for fish to eat.]
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Deer captured in bird-net: water flows upstream. One partner claims a deer he has captured in his bird-net. The other pretends to be watching water flow upstream.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: One partner claims a deer he has captured in his bird-net. The other pretends to be watching water flow upstream.

name: Ruler's absurdity rebuked.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Absurdity of entrusting military mission to bishop. Courtier rebukes king by asking that he be given an ecclesiastical post.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Courtier rebukes king by asking that he be given an ecclesiastical post.

name: Absurdity of trying to convert king to Hinduism – like making cow of donkey or black dog into white.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: KASHMIR . Jammu : Brown Tawi MS No. 20. - HIMA- LAYA . Upreti Proverbs 315. - MADRAS . Natesa Sastri Tennalirama 40f .

name: One absurdity rebukes another – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Priest forbidden to have female servant ostentatiously washes his own clothes. Bishop reverses the order.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Bishop reverses the order.

name: Scholar given third egg. A scholar, showing his skill in logic, proves that two chickens (or eggs) on the table are really three. His father (or host) takes one chicken for himself, gives the other to the mother, tells son that he can have the third one.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A scholar, showing his skill in logic, proves that two chickens (or eggs) on the table are really three. His father (or host) takes one chicken for himself, gives the other to the mother, tells son that he can have the third one.

name: Retorts between husband and wife. (Cf. J1532.1.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.

name: Husband outwits his wife.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.

name: The good words. A man pledged to give his wife only good words hits her with a prayer book.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
additional_description: A man pledged to give his wife only good words hits her with a prayer book.

name: Sharing joy and sorrow. Man pledged to do so with his wife follows instructions literally. Beats her – a joy to him and a sorrow to her.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
additional_description: Man pledged to do so with his wife follows instructions literally. Beats her a joy to him and a sorrow to her.

name: The flute makes more noise. A man overcomes his quarrelsome wife by playing the flute as long as she is scolding.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Bebel I 228 No. 139; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: A man overcomes his quarrelsome wife by playing the flute as long as she is scolding.

name: Woman repulses ugly husband's advances. He placates her by telling her that he wants a handsome child.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
additional_description: He placates her by telling her that he wants a handsome child.

name: Husband and wife separate and divide property. Man keeps the house, gives the wife the road.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
additional_description: Man keeps the house, gives the wife the road.

name: Wife outwits her husband.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.

name: Will work when beaten. A wife whose husband has beaten her sends a rumor to the sick king that her husband is a skilled physician but will practice only when he is well beaten. He is seized and whipped.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
bibliographies: Wesselski Mönchslatein 117 No. 98; Mensa Philosophica No. 73.
additional_description: A wife whose husband has beaten her sends a rumor to the sick king that her husband is a skilled physician but will practice only when he is well beaten. He is seized and whipped.

name: Four men's mistress. A husband disguises as a priest to hear his wife's confession. She says that she has been mistress of a servant, a knight, a fool, and a priest; i.e., her husband when he was her servant, and later her knight. He had then been a fool for demanding her confession, and was a priest because he had heard it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 793; Wesselski Mönchslatein 109 No. 93; Boccaccio Decameron VII No. 5 (Lee 198); Scala Celi 49a No. 275; *Hibbard 41 n. 12; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No 78; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: A husband disguises as a priest to hear his wife's confession. She says that she has been mistress of a servant, a knight, a fool, and a priest; i.e., her husband when he was her servant, and later her knight. He had then been a fool for demanding her confession, and was a priest because he had heard it.

name: Fault-finding husband nonplussed. The wife has cooked so many dishes that when he complains, she can always supply another. Finally he says, "I had rather eat dung." She produces some.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Theorie 175; Plattdeutsch: Wisser Plattdeutsche Volksmärchen (Jena 1922, 1927) II 98; India (Kashmir): Knowles 245.
additional_description: The wife has cooked so many dishes that when he complains, she can always supply another. Finally he says, "I had rather eat dung." She produces some.

name: Which does the more work. Wife shows that she does many more tasks in one morning than the husband.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Santal : Bodding II 253ff . No. 59 .
additional_description: Wife shows that she does many more tasks in one morning than the husband.

name: Where are the shoes? Fault-finding husband threatens to hit wife with shoes. She: "You will have to have some shoes first."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
additional_description: Fault-finding husband threatens to hit wife with shoes. She: "You will have to have some shoes first."

name: Man who continually threatens to leave wife mortified when she tells him to go; pretends his buffalo is dragging him home (after he does leave).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.

name: The exiled wife's dearest possession. A wife driven from home is allowed by her husband to take her one dearest possession. She takes her sleeping husband and effects reconciliation.

additional_description: A wife driven from home is allowed by her husband to take her one dearest possession. She takes her sleeping husband and effects reconciliation.

additional_description: (Women of Weinsberg.) Permitted to carry from the city their dearest possession, they take their husbands.

name: Husband tells wife in indecent posture to "lock up shop". She retorts that he has the key.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.

name: Corpse to be cut in two for easy carrying. Husband who feigns death hears wife propose this. He upbraids her; she replies: "If you had really died I should have given myself up to be burnt."

section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: BOMBAY . Marathi : Man- waring 194 .
additional_description: Husband who feigns death hears wife propose this. He upbraids her; she replies: "If you had really died I should have given myself up to be burnt."

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.
additional_description: Husband declares that he will drown himself. The wife shows him a deep spot in the stream.

name: Overcurious wife learns of the senate's deliberations. Husband (son) tells her that they have decided that each man may have many wives. Gullible wife believes it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1540. Retorts between husband and wife.

name: Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.

name: Imaginary debt and payment.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Imagined intercourse, imagined payment. A woman demands money for a visit which she dreams of having had from a merchant. She is shown the money in a mirror.

section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.

name: Imagined penance for imagined sin. A penitent confesses that a plan to sin had entered his mind. Priest tells him that the thought is as good as the deed. Assesses four florins as penance. Penitent says that he had only had it in his mind to give the florins; he must take the thought for the deed.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 298; *Wesselski Gonnella 110 No. 10; *Fischer-Bolte Reise der Söhne Giaffers 210.
additional_description: A penitent confesses that a plan to sin had entered his mind. Priest tells him that the thought is as good as the deed. Assesses four florins as penance. Penitent says that he had only had it in his mind to give the florins; he must take the thought for the deed.

name: Singer repaid with promise of reward: words for words.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
bibliographies: *Fischer-Bolte Reise der Söhne Giaffers 211; Nouvelles Récréations No. 3; Hindu: Penzer V 132.

name: Directions for getting pay given in return for directions for healing.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.

name: Substitute for candle repaid with substitute for money. A monk gives a man a stick instead of the candle the man has wanted to burn before a holy picture. The monk says that it will have the same effect as if the candle were burned. The man takes out his purse and lets the monk touch it.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: A monk gives a man a stick instead of the candle the man has wanted to burn before a holy picture. The monk says that it will have the same effect as if the candle were burned. The man takes out his purse and lets the monk touch it.

name: The hare at third remove. A man receives a present of a hare. Later a crowd comes to him for entertainment saying that they are friends of the man who presented the hare. This happens a second time. He serves them clear water. "It is the soup from the soup of the hare."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: A man receives a present of a hare. Later a crowd comes to him for entertainment saying that they are friends of the man who presented the hare. This happens a second time. He serves them clear water. "It is the soup from the soup of the hare."

name: Imagined ownership: derived from a dream. Man claims ownership of bulls because he has dreamed of them. He is given their shadows.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: Man claims ownership of bulls because he has dreamed of them. He is given their shadows.

name: Imagined color. Clerk tells person to imagine that blue cloth is green. The customer walks out without paying. The clerk asks for payment; the customer tells clerk to imagine he has been paid.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: Clerk tells person to imagine that blue cloth is green. The customer walks out without paying. The clerk asks for payment; the customer tells clerk to imagine he has been paid.

name: Half of money thrown into tank. The monkey to the grocer: "You sold half water and half milk."

section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: U.P. Oudh : Crooke - Rouse 213 No. 33 .
additional_description: The monkey to the grocer: "You sold half water and half milk."

name: The priest administers to a man sick with infectious disease. Shows him the Host through window, and says: "Have hope and imagine you receive it." The man shows the priest a coin through the window, and repeats the same phrase.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: Shows him the Host through window, and says: "Have hope and imagine you receive it." The man shows the priest a coin through the window, and repeats the same phrase.

name: "Here is half of picture and you must imagine other half." Jester later redecorates house in fragments of pictures.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: Jester later redecorates house in fragments of pictures.

name: Loans refused.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.

name: The ass consulted about the loan. A man wants to borrow an ass. The owner goes to see what the ass says. The ass is unwilling.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: A man wants to borrow an ass. The owner goes to see what the ass says. The ass is unwilling.

name: The ass is not at home. A man wants to borrow an ass. The owner says that the ass is not at home. The ass brays and the borrower protests. "Will you believe an ass and not a graybeard like me?"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 224 No. 65; L. Schmidt Oesterr. Zs. f. Vksk. 1954, 128; Italian Novella: Rotunda.

name: The bigger fool. When told by servants their master is not at home, man says it is a fool that goes out in such midday heat. Whereupon master sticks his head out of the window, saying "Thou who art moving about at this time art the big fool: I have been seated all day in my house."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: KASHMIR . Chitral : Davidson , J. Ind . Antiq . XXIX 247 .
additional_description: When told by servants their master is not at home, man says it is a fool that goes out in such midday heat. Whereupon master sticks his head out of the window, saying "Thou who art moving about at this time art the big fool: I have been seated all day in my house."

name: Three reasons for refusing credit. A man asks for credit, although he has always paid cash before. He is refused on these grounds: either (1) he has never found anyone to trust him, (2) he has never bought anything, or (3) he is rich and does not need an extension of time.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: A man asks for credit, although he has always paid cash before. He is refused on these grounds: either (1) he has never found anyone to trust him, (2) he has never bought anything, or (3) he is rich and does not need an extension of time.

name: Man refuses to lend horse: sued for consequent damages. The would-be borrower gets one from another neighbor. He overworks the horse and renders him useless. The owner of the horse sues the man who had refused to lend his animal. Reasoning: "If he had lent his horse this would not have happened to mine." Settled by compromise.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: The would-be borrower gets one from another neighbor. He overworks the horse and renders him useless. The owner of the horse sues the man who had refused to lend his animal. Reasoning: "If he had lent his horse this would not have happened to mine." Settled by compromise.

name: Better to donate half of what is asked than lend all. Two farmers ask a priest to lend two measures of grain to each of them. The priest refuses to lend them any but donates one measure to each. Thus he saves two measures.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: Two farmers ask a priest to lend two measures of grain to each of them. The priest refuses to lend them any but donates one measure to each. Thus he saves two measures.

name: Lender repays borrower for deceptive loan.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: CENT . INDIA . Bastar : Elwin ( 1944 ) 237 .

name: Miscellaneous retorts concerning borrowing and lending.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.

name: A present or a retaining fee. An abbot presents a lawyer with a fine horse. Later the abbot comes to Rome and calls on the lawyer for help. The latter returns the horse. "I did not know that you had a lawsuit in Rome."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: An abbot presents a lawyer with a fine horse. Later the abbot comes to Rome and calls on the lawyer for help. The latter returns the horse. "I did not know that you had a lawsuit in Rome."

name: God as surety; the abbot pays. A young man is ransomed by giving God as surety for the ransom money. He fails to return as agreed. The creditor sees a wealthy abbot, who says that he is a servant of God. He robs the abbot and when the young man finally appears he tells the latter than the debt is already paid by God's servant.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: A young man is ransomed by giving God as surety for the ransom money. He fails to return as agreed. The creditor sees a wealthy abbot, who says that he is a servant of God. He robs the abbot and when the young man finally appears he tells the latter than the debt is already paid by God's servant.

name: Too large a payment. A student leaving the university sends back a small coin to pay for the knowledge he is carrying away, although he says that he is really paying too much.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1550. Practical retorts: borrowers and lenders.
additional_description: A student leaving the university sends back a small coin to pay for the knowledge he is carrying away, although he says that he is really paying too much.

name: Turning the plate around. A cuts the meat and puts all the good things on his side. B turns the plate around: "See how all things turn about in this world." A turns it back: "However the world may turn, with good friends like us the plate will always remain the same."

section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.

name: Priest frightens away parasitic guests. Tells them he has that morning confessed man with plague.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
additional_description: Tells them he has that morning confessed man with plague.

name: Talker keeps person from eating.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.

name: Trickster's interrupted feast revenged. He is asked a question by his master each time he tries to eat a date, so that he always has to spit out the date. He revenges himself that night when the master tries to carry on an intrigue with his wife's maid. He comes with his answers at embarrassing moments.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
additional_description: He is asked a question by his master each time he tries to eat a date, so that he always has to spit out the date. He revenges himself that night when the master tries to carry on an intrigue with his wife's maid. He comes with his answers at embarrassing moments.

name: Revenge by interrupting feast. A rabbi who has been inhospitably treated is afterwards invited to dinner. He keeps the guests so amused by his jokes that they fail to eat and the feast is spoiled.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
additional_description: A rabbi who has been inhospitably treated is afterwards invited to dinner. He keeps the guests so amused by his jokes that they fail to eat and the feast is spoiled.

name: Inappropriate entertainment repaid.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.

name: Fox and crane invite each other. Fox serves the food on a flat dish so that the crane cannot eat. Crane serves his food in a bottle.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
bibliographies: *Type 60; Wienert FFC LVI 54 (ET 141), 98 (ST 123); Halm Aesop No. 34; *Crane Exempla 202 No. 165; Africa (Mpongwe): Nassau 64 No. 13.
additional_description: Fox serves the food on a flat dish so that the crane cannot eat. Crane serves his food in a bottle.

name: Bugs unable to eat honey at banquet of bees, and bees unable to eat dung at bug's dinner.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.

name: Crop division between parrot and cat: they try to cheat each other by inviting to a dinner in turns every day.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: U.P. Oudh : Crooke - Rouse 186ff . No. 43

name: Luxury of host rebuked.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.

name: Philosopher spits in king's beard. It is the only place he can find at the royal table not covered with gold and jewels.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
bibliographies: *Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 475; *Crane Vitry 195 No. 149; Scala Celi 140a No. 784; *Krappe Bulletin Hispanique XXXIX 29, Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda.
additional_description: It is the only place he can find at the royal table not covered with gold and jewels.

name: Ruler refuses hospitality from subject who spends more than he earns.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.

name: Host rebukes negligent servant.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.

name: A step-ladder for setting the table. Servant who leaves off the salt is instructed to bring in the step-ladder so as to see what is missing.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
additional_description: Servant who leaves off the salt is instructed to bring in the step-ladder so as to see what is missing.

name: Guest brings along cakes to eat. Stingy host rebuked.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
bibliographies: Spanish: Espinosa III No. 192; Italian Novella: *Rotunda, Boccaccio Decameron I No. 8 (Lee 23).
additional_description: Stingy host rebuked.

name: Suitor brings own lamp. Mother of girl tells suitor that she cannot have him burning her midnight oil. The next night he brings his own lamp and a can of oil, tells her he will stay all night if he wishes.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
additional_description: Mother of girl tells suitor that she cannot have him burning her midnight oil. The next night he brings his own lamp and a can of oil, tells her he will stay all night if he wishes.

name: "Cause liberality to be depicted." Answer of hungry man when host asks for suggestion for a picture to be painted of something that has not been seen.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
bibliographies: Boccaccio Decameron I No. 8 (Lee 23), Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: Answer of hungry man when host asks for suggestion for a picture to be painted of something that has not been seen.

name: Deceptive invitation to feast.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.

name: Inviting to a feast only those whose households have never been touched by death. None comes.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1560. Practical retorts: hosts and guests.
additional_description: None comes.

name: Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.

name: Stingy almsgiving repaid.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.

name: Poem for poem: all for all. A poet gives the emperor a poem, hoping for a reward The emperor later gives the poet a poem in return. The poet thereupon sends the emperor two pennies, saying that this is all the money he has. The emperor, seeing that he is defeated in the exchange, laughs and becomes the poet's friend.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.
additional_description: A poet gives the emperor a poem, hoping for a reward The emperor later gives the poet a poem in return. The poet thereupon sends the emperor two pennies, saying that this is all the money he has. The emperor, seeing that he is defeated in the exchange, laughs and becomes the poet's friend.

name: Reinforcement of the request for alms. A clown asks for alms and receives nothing. He then throws pellets made of herbs at the steward, saying that there is great power in words, herbs, and stones: he has tried two of them and plans to try the third. He is given alms.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.
additional_description: A clown asks for alms and receives nothing. He then throws pellets made of herbs at the steward, saying that there is great power in words, herbs, and stones: he has tried two of them and plans to try the third. He is given alms.

name: Base money in the offering.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.

name: The penny baked in the wafer. A peasant always puts a bad penny into the offering. The priest has a penny baked in a wafer and gives it to the peasant at communion. The peasant, unable to swallow it, thinks that he is possessed of the devil. The priest asks whether he has ever done wrong with a penny, secures confession and a pledge of reform.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.
additional_description: A peasant always puts a bad penny into the offering. The priest has a penny baked in a wafer and gives it to the peasant at communion. The peasant, unable to swallow it, thinks that he is possessed of the devil. The priest asks whether he has ever done wrong with a penny, secures confession and a pledge of reform.

name: Sulphur in the censer. Parishioners put base money into the collection. The priest burns sulphur instead of incense He tells them that their money will buy nothing better.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.
additional_description: Parishioners put base money into the collection. The priest burns sulphur instead of incense He tells them that their money will buy nothing better.

name: Interested only in his capital. Preacher tells parishioners (regarding the day's offering): "You will get back 100 for 1." Donor of a penny: "I'll be glad to get back my capital!"
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.
additional_description: Preacher tells parishioners (regarding the day's offering): "You will get back 100 for 1." Donor of a penny: "I'll be glad to get back my capital!"

name: Any boon desired. Stingy king insists on trickster asking any boon desired. By asking king to perform disgusting act, trickster compels king to give expensive gift.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
section: J1580. Practical retorts connected with almsgiving.
additional_description: Stingy king insists on trickster asking any boon desired. By asking king to perform disgusting act, trickster compels king to give expensive gift.

name: Practical retorts – miscellaneous.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: How much the ass cost. As the fool brings the new ass home everyone wants to know how much it cost. He has all publicly assemble and announces just what it cost.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Spanish: Boggs FFC XC 134 No. 1550C, Espinosa No. 54.
additional_description: As the fool brings the new ass home everyone wants to know how much it cost. He has all publicly assemble and announces just what it cost.

name: Throw at a rich man. Philosopher gives a penny to a man who throws stones at him but advises him that it will be wiser to throw at one who can afford to give more. The advice is followed and the rascal is arrested and hanged.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Philosopher gives a penny to a man who throws stones at him but advises him that it will be wiser to throw at one who can afford to give more. The advice is followed and the rascal is arrested and hanged.

name: Eyes treated for the stomach ache. A man who has stomach ache from eating bad bread is treated for his eyes so that he will thereafter be able to tell good bread from bad. [Inadvertant duplication of X372.3.]
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man who has stomach ache from eating bad bread is treated for his eyes so that he will thereafter be able to tell good bread from bad.

name: The fish in the sleeve. A particularly obnoxious man delays others while he haggles over prices with a butcher. Meanwhile a parson takes the three fish the man has laid down and puts them into his large sleeves. The man is angry and searches in vain for them. The parson then shows them and advises him not to let people cheat him.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A particularly obnoxious man delays others while he haggles over prices with a butcher. Meanwhile a parson takes the three fish the man has laid down and puts them into his large sleeves. The man is angry and searches in vain for them. The parson then shows them and advises him not to let people cheat him.

name: One wrong and five hundred good deeds. A man steals a large sum, keeps half and distributes the rest among five hundred persons. He says that he has committed one wrong but has done five hundred good deeds.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man steals a large sum, keeps half and distributes the rest among five hundred persons. He says that he has committed one wrong but has done five hundred good deeds.

name: Two monks renew their appetites. Entertained by a lord, they say that they are going to certain waters to recover their appetites. They are taken to a chamber and locked in for a day They recover their appetites without further journeying. (Cf. K1955.1.1.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Köhler-Bolte I 137, II 5714; *Anderson FFC XLII 359; Boccaccio Decameron X No. 2 (Lee 311ff.); Scala Celi 126a Nos. 685, 686; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: Entertained by a lord, they say that they are going to certain waters to recover their appetites. They are taken to a chamber and locked in for a day They recover their appetites without further journeying.

name: Hungry man eats intestines of fish next morning after refusing to do so the evening before.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The testament of the dog. The owner of a dog has him given Christian burial. The bishop thereupon pretends that the dog has left the church a large legacy.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Anderson FFC XLII 359 n 3; Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 72; **Feilberg "Hundens Testament" Festskrift til E. T. Kristensen 11ff.; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; Cent Nouvelles Nouvelles No. 96.
additional_description: The owner of a dog has him given Christian burial. The bishop thereupon pretends that the dog has left the church a large legacy.

name: Ass's charter in his hoof. The ass absents himself from the parliament of beasts. The lion sends the fox and the wolf to summon him. He pleads his charter of exemption and invites the fox to read it in his hoof. (Cf. K551.18.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Herbert III 53; Crane Vitry 147 No. 33; Baum MLN XXXVII 350; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: The ass absents himself from the parliament of beasts. The lion sends the fox and the wolf to summon him. He pleads his charter of exemption and invites the fox to read it in his hoof.

name: The stolen meat and the weighed cat. A man buys three pounds of meat. His wife eats it and says that the cat ate it. The man weighs the cat and finds that it does not weigh three pounds.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Type 1373; Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 232 No. 87, *II 185 No. 348.
additional_description: A man buys three pounds of meat. His wife eats it and says that the cat ate it. The man weighs the cat and finds that it does not weigh three pounds.

name: The lazy ass repaid in kind. Loaded with salt, he falls down in the river and lightens his burden. His master then loads him with sponges so that the next time the ass tries the trick he increases his load.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Type 211***; Wienert FFC LVI 72 (ET 372), 98 (ST 126); Halm Aesop No. 322.
additional_description: Loaded with salt, he falls down in the river and lightens his burden. His master then loads him with sponges so that the next time the ass tries the trick he increases his load.

name: The rescuers' Sabbath. A Jew, fallen into a pit, refuses to be rescued on Saturday, his Sabbath. The next day he calls for help but the Christians refuse to rescue him on Sunday, their Sabbath.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wesselski Mönchslatein 100 No 84; Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 389.
additional_description: A Jew, fallen into a pit, refuses to be rescued on Saturday, his Sabbath. The next day he calls for help but the Christians refuse to rescue him on Sunday, their Sabbath.

name: The forehanded servant. A parson boasts that when he asks his maid if certain work is done she always answers that it has been done long ago. A guest wagers that she can be trapped if she is asked whether she has thrown the parson's suit of clothes into the tub of water. She overhears the wager and has the suit in the water before he asks the question.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A parson boasts that when he asks his maid if certain work is done she always answers that it has been done long ago. A guest wagers that she can be trapped if she is asked whether she has thrown the parson's suit of clothes into the tub of water. She overhears the wager and has the suit in the water before he asks the question.

name: That which was promised him. A tenant promises his daughter to his master against her will. The master sends for "that which was promised him." The daughter sends the horse, and it is taken into the master's chamber.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A tenant promises his daughter to his master against her will. The master sends for "that which was promised him." The daughter sends the horse, and it is taken into the master's chamber.

name: St. Peter not guiltless. Soldiers are admitted neither to hell nor to heaven. They remind Peter of his denial of Christ. He admits them.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Soldiers are admitted neither to hell nor to heaven. They remind Peter of his denial of Christ. He admits them.

name: God's omniscience. A woman tells her brother of God's omniscience. When he is ill and the priest has been called, he creeps under some straw and hides. The priest leaves. The man: "If God were so omniscient he might have found me for the priest."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A woman tells her brother of God's omniscience. When he is ill and the priest has been called, he creeps under some straw and hides. The priest leaves. The man: "If God were so omniscient he might have found me for the priest."

name: The one word petition. A guardian of monks in need of food approaches the prince The prince is busy and says that he will permit the guardian but a single word. The guardian says, "Soup", and secures all the food he wants from the prince.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A guardian of monks in need of food approaches the prince The prince is busy and says that he will permit the guardian but a single word. The guardian says, "Soup", and secures all the food he wants from the prince.

name: Sharing his wounds. Two men on parting agree to share everything they receive. One returns with much money. His friend reminds him of the pact. He replies that he has also received many wounds. The friend is willing to forego dividing.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Two men on parting agree to share everything they receive. One returns with much money. His friend reminds him of the pact. He replies that he has also received many wounds. The friend is willing to forego dividing.

name: The blacksmith's and the horse-trader's dreams. They are forced to sleep together because of lack of room. The blacksmith tells the horsetrader that he often dreams that he is striking the bellows and that he may accidently strike him in the night. The horsetrader retaliates by dreaming that he is riding a horse and drives the spurs into the blacksmith.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: They are forced to sleep together because of lack of room. The blacksmith tells the horsetrader that he often dreams that he is striking the bellows and that he may accidently strike him in the night. The horsetrader retaliates by dreaming that he is riding a horse and drives the spurs into the blacksmith.

name: Drunkard cured of seeing double. When he claims to see two roast chickens, his wife takes the one chicken away and he falls into the fire trying to find the other.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: When he claims to see two roast chickens, his wife takes the one chicken away and he falls into the fire trying to find the other.

name: The priest makes the omen come true. A woman crosses herself when she meets a priest, as if he were the devil. She says that meeting a priest always brings her bad luck. The priest knocks her down and steps on her. "As ye believe, so shall it be."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A woman crosses herself when she meets a priest, as if he were the devil. She says that meeting a priest always brings her bad luck. The priest knocks her down and steps on her. "As ye believe, so shall it be."

name: Armies like seeds and peppercorns. One king sends large sack of seed to the other to represent the number of his soldiers. The second replies with a small bag of peppercorns: "My army is small compared to yours but has all the power of the peppercorn compared to your lifeless seed."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: One king sends large sack of seed to the other to represent the number of his soldiers. The second replies with a small bag of peppercorns: "My army is small compared to yours but has all the power of the peppercorn compared to your lifeless seed."

name: The sound of the harp. An apprentice harpmaker is blamed that he has not made the harp sound. He throws the harp at his master's head and breaks it to pieces: "There you have your sound."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: An apprentice harpmaker is blamed that he has not made the harp sound. He throws the harp at his master's head and breaks it to pieces: "There you have your sound."

name: To give the accounting afterward. A nobleman embezzles money from the public treasury. When counsellors urge an accounting, he replies that he is ready as soon as they have themselves made an accounting of all their transactions. They are ready to let well enough alone.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A nobleman embezzles money from the public treasury. When counsellors urge an accounting, he replies that he is ready as soon as they have themselves made an accounting of all their transactions. They are ready to let well enough alone.

name: Taking cold in effigy. A traitor is hanged in effigy naked. Later the citizens sue for peace and arrange a meeting. They find him in a hot room swathed with many blankets. He says that he has taken cold when the night wind blew on him as he hung naked from the gallows.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A traitor is hanged in effigy naked. Later the citizens sue for peace and arrange a meeting. They find him in a hot room swathed with many blankets. He says that he has taken cold when the night wind blew on him as he hung naked from the gallows.

name: An expensive joke. A shoemaker's apprentice greases boots as he would grease a fowl. The owner in anger returns and breaks a window.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A shoemaker's apprentice greases boots as he would grease a fowl. The owner in anger returns and breaks a window.

name: The minstrel repays the cobbler. The cobbler learns the minstrel's songs and sings them so that the minstrel loses his trade. He in turn sews the cobbler's leather into crazy shapes. He has done no worse than the cobbler, who marred his songs. When they sing together the people realize the contrast and patronize the minstrel.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Spanish: Boggs FFC XC 139 No. 1695A*, Keller.
additional_description: The cobbler learns the minstrel's songs and sings them so that the minstrel loses his trade. He in turn sews the cobbler's leather into crazy shapes. He has done no worse than the cobbler, who marred his songs. When they sing together the people realize the contrast and patronize the minstrel.

name: Brotherly love and patience both dead. A man failing to receive alms in a village, rings the bell for mass. He tells the sexton that he is ringing because of the death of brotherly love. The sexton rings another bell. It is for the death of the other man's patience.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man failing to receive alms in a village, rings the bell for mass. He tells the sexton that he is ringing because of the death of brotherly love. The sexton rings another bell. It is for the death of the other man's patience.

name: To follow the king. In order to test a favorite, a king says that he is going to retire from the world and offers the regency to the favorite. On advice from his philosopher, the favorite says that he is going to accompany the king into retirement.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Chauvin II 148 No. 1, III 98 No. 2; Spanish Exempla: Keller.
additional_description: In order to test a favorite, a king says that he is going to retire from the world and offers the regency to the favorite. On advice from his philosopher, the favorite says that he is going to accompany the king into retirement.

name: Robber gives priest double his penance and then takes his horse.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Mensa Philosophica No. 53; Shakespeare Jest Books III (Certayne Conceyts and Jeasts) 11 No. 22.

name: He has a family to support. Ruler to servant stealing his game: "If you wish any I'll send you some."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Ruler to servant stealing his game: "If you wish any I'll send you some."

name: Sticking to the rules. Merchant at inn deprives monk of fowl. "It's against the rules of your order." Later the monk undertakes to help merchant across stream. In the middle of the stream he asks the merchant if he has any money. "Yes." The monk drops him in the water. "It's against the rules of our order to have any money on us."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Merchant at inn deprives monk of fowl. "It's against the rules of your order." Later the monk undertakes to help merchant across stream. In the middle of the stream he asks the merchant if he has any money. "Yes." The monk drops him in the water. "It's against the rules of our order to have any money on us."

name: Priest asked to preach short sermon does so saying to congregation only that they are wicked.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Do not start here. Man asks directions to a certain place. Native attempts to give directions, then in disgust says, "If I wanted to go to –, I wouldn't start from here."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Man asks directions to a certain place. Native attempts to give directions, then in disgust says, "If I wanted to go to   , I wouldn't start from here."

name: The abbot cannot find his needle. An undesirable abbot furnishes adequate grounds for his dismissal when he cannot find the needle that all monks are supposed to carry with them. If careless in little things he will be careless in great.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: An undesirable abbot furnishes adequate grounds for his dismissal when he cannot find the needle that all monks are supposed to carry with them. If careless in little things he will be careless in great.

name: Kissing the mother first. Oracle that the first of three sons to kiss his mother will be king. One of them kisses the earth, mother of all. He succeeds.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Oracle that the first of three sons to kiss his mother will be king. One of them kisses the earth, mother of all. He succeeds.

name: Using the lamb to get an audience. Peasant calls on lawyer for advice. Lawyer says that he is busy. Peasant returns with lamb. The lawyer hears its bleat and grants the audience.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Peasant calls on lawyer for advice. Lawyer says that he is busy. Peasant returns with lamb. The lawyer hears its bleat and grants the audience.

name: Clever ways of concealing jewels (treasure).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Jewels concealed in cowdung cakes.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: INDIA . Venkataswami 48.- KASHMIR . Knowles Tales 206 ; Jammu : Brown Tawi MS No. 76 .

name: Valuable rubies baked in bread.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Coins concealed in jar of oil (pickles).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: BOMBAY . Marathi : Dexter 41f . No. 14 .

name: Clever deductions.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Deduction : one - eye , long - bearded thief named Kale Khan.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: U.P. Etah : NQ V No. 3 .

name: Deductions from observation.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Deduction: the one-eyed camel. A she-camel has passed, blind in one eye; on the one side she carries wine and on the other vinegar; two men lead her, one a heathen and the other a Jew. Solution: She is recognized as a she-camel by the footprints; she is blind because she feeds on only one side of the road; the wine dropping down has soaked into the earth; the vinegar makes bubbles; the heathen is not so careful in his manners as is the Jew.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: **Fischer-Bolte Reise der Söhne Giaffers 198ff.; Gaster Exempla 195 No. 51; *Penzer VI 287; Italian Novella: Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: HIMALAYA . Burhabhakat 147-50 . - BOMBAY . Grierson Survey VII ( 1 ) 277f . , 446f . - MADRAS . Tamil : King- scote 141ff .; Korean: Zong in-Sob 101 No. 55.
additional_description: A she-camel has passed, blind in one eye; on the one side she carries wine and on the other vinegar; two men lead her, one a heathen and the other a Jew. Solution: She is recognized as a she-camel by the footprints; she is blind because she feeds on only one side of the road; the wine dropping down has soaked into the earth; the vinegar makes bubbles; the heathen is not so careful in his manners as is the Jew.

name: Deduction: the camel ridden by a pregnant woman.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: U.P . Mirzapur : NQ III No. 175 .

name: Deduction: the king is a bastard. After dinner the king begins to dance. He is therefore called illegitimate. His mother acknowledges an intrigue with a dancer.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Type 655; *Fischer-Bolte Reise der Söhne Giaffers 198ff.; *Gaster Exempla 195 No. 51; Herrmann Saxo II 265ff.; Spanish Exempla: Keller; Italian Novella: *Rotunda; India: Thompson-Balys: PUNJAB . King Folk - Lore XXXVI 364f.-U.P. Mirzapur : NQ III No. 175
additional_description: After dinner the king begins to dance. He is therefore called illegitimate. His mother acknowledges an intrigue with a dancer.

name: Deduction: magistrate is a bastard.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Deduction: bread made by a sick woman. It falls apart; therefore it was kneaded by a weak person.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: King's capriciousness censured: the ass in the stream. A nobleman seeing an ass letting water in a river remarks that it reminds him of his king. He explains to the king that just as the ass puts water where it is already plentiful, so the king awards wealth where it is not needed. The king says that it is all in the nature of the nobleman's fate. Subsequent events prove this.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Pauli (ed. Bolte) No. 836; Boccaccio Decameron X No. 1; Italian Novella: Rotunda.
additional_description: A nobleman seeing an ass letting water in a river remarks that it reminds him of his king. He explains to the king that just as the ass puts water where it is already plentiful, so the king awards wealth where it is not needed. The king says that it is all in the nature of the nobleman's fate. Subsequent events prove this.

name: Son of God to see king. When steadily unable to be admitted to presence of a king, a clever man demands an interview saying he is the son of God come down to earth and will condescend to see the king. When asked to show the king Heaven and the path to it, the pretender retorts that when he was sent down by the Father, he was told to look to matters relating to this earth.

additional_description: When steadily unable to be admitted to presence of a king, a clever man demands an interview saying he is the son of God come down to earth and will condescend to see the king. bibliographies: *Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 210 No. 23; U.S.: Baughman.
additional_description: As he is making water he hears a brook flowing and mistakes what it is. He waits for a day and a half.

name: Did the calf eat the man? A fool, liking the shoes on the feet of a man hanged on a gallows, cuts off the swollen feet in order to carry off the shoes. In the room in which he sleeps that night is a newborn calf. The next morning the man takes the shoes but leaves the feet. Peasants agree that the calf has eaten the man all but the feet. They burn the house to destroy the calf.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Type 1281; *Wesselski Bebel I 231 No. 144; Danish: Christensen DF L 65, Nyrop Dania I (1890) 283ff., cf. II 68ff.; U.S.: Baughman.
additional_description: A fool, liking the shoes on the feet of a man hanged on a gallows, cuts off the swollen feet in order to carry off the shoes. In the room in which he sleeps that night is a newborn calf. The next morning the man takes the shoes but leaves the feet. Peasants agree that the calf has eaten the man all but the feet. They burn the house to destroy the calf.

name: Christ's image has broken his arm. A man refuses to have anything to do with the crucifix because once an image of Christ has fallen on him in church and broken his arm. Reminded that this image is not the same, he replies that it is the son of the other and is just as bad.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Which way the sheep shall return. One man plans to buy sheep; another says that he shall not drive them across the bridge. They quarrel over the sheep, which have not yet been acquired. A third numskull to convince them of their foolishness pours all his meal out in the water so as to show them the empty sack. "How much meal is in the sack?" he asks. "None." "There is just that much wit in your heads."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: *Clouston Noodles 26; Field Pent Cuckoo 2; England: Baughman.
additional_description: One man plans to buy sheep; another says that he shall not drive them across the bridge. They quarrel over the sheep, which have not yet been acquired. "There is just that much wit in your heads."
