name: The polluted clothes. A Brahmin washes clothes to purify them. As they hang to dry, a dog walks under them and the Brahmin fears that they are polluted. By putting himself on all-fours like a dog and fastening a leaf like a dog's tail he experiments and decides that the dog must have touched the clothes. He therefore destroys them instead of rewashing them.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A Brahmin washes clothes to purify them. As they hang to dry, a dog walks under them and the Brahmin fears that they are polluted. He therefore destroys them instead of rewashing them.

name: The man who wanted to be dead one day. A husband tells his wife that he has provisions for every day in the year but one. The man then thinks to frighten them by rising from the dead. One servant thinking the dead man suffering from devils kills him.

name: Logically absurd defenses. Thief brought to judgment for breaking into house blames mason for building poor house. Mason blames maker of mortar, who blames potter, who blames pretty woman who diverted his attention. She blames goldsmith who caused her to go for her earrings. Goldsmith has no one to blame but he is too old to make a good execution. Hence a shopkeeper across the way is convicted.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Clouston Noodles 87; *Wesselski Hessische Blätter f. Vksk. XXXII 23; India: Thompson-Balys: KASHMIR . Jammu : Brown Tawi MS No. 33. GANGES VALLEY . Shyama - Shankar 155-6 . - U.P . Mirzapur : NQ III No. 454.- BENGAL . Banerji 6-14 . MADRAS . Natesa Sastri Folk - Tales 343ff . - CEYLON . Sinhalese : Goonetilleke , Wm . Orientalist I 191f . ( two versions ) ; Sinhalese : Lewis Orientalist I 190-91 .
additional_description: Thief brought to judgment for breaking into house blames mason for building poor house. Mason blames maker of mortar, who blames potter, who blames pretty woman who diverted his attention. She blames goldsmith who caused her to go for her earrings. Goldsmith has no one to blame but he is too old to make a good execution. Hence a shopkeeper across the way is convicted.

They meet a busy man whom they try to persuade to join them. He pleads business. The "dead" man speaks: "It's no use, friend, to try to dissuade them."

He is beaten by mule-drivers when he tells them that he is a dead man. He returns home and tells his wife that he returns from the dead. "How goes it in heaven?" "For one thing, avoid mule-drivers."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

He is beaten by mule-drivers when he tells them that he is a dead man. He returns home and tells his wife that he returns from the dead. " How goes it in heaven? " "For one thing, avoid mule-drivers.

name: Foolish boasts get man into trouble. Man boasts to boss that his brother can do twice the work he does. The boss hires him. chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The philosophical watchman. A master sets his servant to keep watch over his horse at night. He soon asks the servant if he is asleep. "No, I was thinking of who created so many stars in the sky." The second time the servant answers, "No, I was thinking of who dug the sea. Where did he put the soil?" The third time: "I was wondering who would carry the saddle now that the horse is stolen."

name: Peasants want a living God. An artist, ordered to make a crucifix, asks peasants if they want a living God. They say yes. "If we don't like him we can kill him ourselves."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: An artist, ordered to make a crucifix, asks peasants if they want a living God. They say yes. " If we don't like him we can kill him ourselves.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: "I don't know" thought to be a person's name. A man in a country that cannot speak his language hears "I don't know" so frequently that he thinks it must be the name of the owner of all the places he inquires about.

name: The cup with two and three handles. When the servant girl presents the cup to the emperor she holds the handle herself. When there are two handles she holds one in each hand, and when there are three she holds the third one toward her.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: When the servant girl presents the cup to the emperor she holds the handle herself. When there are two handles she holds one in each hand, and when there are three she holds the third one toward her.

name: Servant injures master while shampooing him. Gives him a slap and breaks his skin.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: Gives him a slap and breaks his skin.

name: The bungling speaker.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: "I am as good as you are." One woman says to another, "You are an adulteress and a thief, and I know that I am just as good as you are."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: "  One woman says to another, "You are an adulteress and a thief, and I know that I am just as good as you are.

name: The fool testifies: "I had been drinking and was lying on the bench asleep. I saw him hit him over the head with an axe but don't know whether he hit him or not."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: I saw him hit him over the head with an axe but don't know whether he hit him or not.

name: The fool betrays his brothers. They escape but he is captured and stupidly betrays their hiding places.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: They escape but he is captured and stupidly betrays their hiding places.

name: The forgetful fool.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: KASHMIR . Knowles Tales 394f . No. 60 .

name: A fox or a hare? A servant is sent by his master to find a girl for him. If he returns with one he is to say, "I have a fox"; if without, "I have a hare." When he returns he has forgotten the agreement. "I don't know whether it is a fox or a hare, but the girl is down stairs.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A servant is sent by his master to find a girl for him. If he returns with one he is to say, "I have a fox"; if without, "I have a hare. " When he returns he has forgotten the agreement. " I don't know whether it is a fox or a hare, but the girl is down stairs.

name: Fool keeps repeating his instructions so as to remember them. (He usually forgets them.)
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: (He usually forgets them.)

name: Fool's talking to himself thought to be inappropriate greetings. He keeps repeating a word to remember it but changes it so that it seems to refer unfavorably to people whom he meets.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: ASSAM VALLEY . Barooah 92ff . No. 12 .
additional_description: He keeps repeating a word to remember it but changes it so that it seems to refer unfavorably to people whom he meets.

name: Bungling fool forgets what elephant is called. Says it is an "earth egg" (confused with name for potatoes).
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: India: Thompson-Balys: GANGES VALLEY . Shyama - Shankar 9f
additional_description: Says it is an "earth egg" (confused with name for potatoes).

name: Foolish messenger muddles message.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: The stolen bedcover. A man hears a noise outside the house at night. He wraps a bed cover about him and goes to investigate. The robbers take the bed cover and flee. The wife asks what the debate was about. "About the bed cover. When they got it, the quarrel was over."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: A man hears a noise outside the house at night. He wraps a bed cover about him and goes to investigate. The robbers take the bed cover and flee. The wife asks what the debate was about. "About the bed cover. When they got it, the quarrel was over."

name: Bungling rescuer caught by crab. He tries to rescue woman caught by crab. Is caught himself and found in embarrassing position.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
bibliographies: Wesselski Hodscha Nasreddin I 178 No. 313; Indonesia: Coster-Wijsman 43 No. 44.
additional_description: He tries to rescue woman caught by crab. Is caught himself and found in embarrassing position.

name: Buyer draws picture of grindstone on his order list. The grocer sends him a cheese. The buyer has forgotten to draw the hole.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
additional_description: The grocer sends him a cheese. The buyer has forgotten to draw the hole.

name: How the tower was built. Fool gives three explanations: (1) It was formerly a deep well; dug up and set out; (2) it was built by a very tall man; (3) it was built flat on the ground and then set up.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division2: J2700–J2749. The easy problem made hard.
section: J2700. The easy problem made hard.

name: "What do I hold in my hand?" (Ring). "In its first state it was in the mountains; it is round and flat." Fool: "A millstone."

section: J2700. The easy problem made hard.

name: The father of Noah's sons. The fool seeks advice as to how to answer this question: Who is the father of Stoffer's three sons? Stoffer, of course. Then who was the father of Noah's three sons? Stoffer.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

section: J2700. The easy problem made hard.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

section: J2700. The easy problem made hard.

name: Why he couldn't see. One fool to another: "What would happen if you cut off your nose?" "I couldn't smell." "What would happen if you cut off your ears?" "I couldn't see." "Why couldn't you see?" "My hat would fall down over my eyes."
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division2: J2700–J2749. The easy problem made hard.
section: J2700. The easy problem made hard.
additional_description: One fool to another: "What would happen if you cut off your nose?" "I couldn't smell." "What would happen if you cut off your ears?" "I couldn't see." "Why couldn't you see?" "My hat would fall down over my eyes."

name: Telling their horses apart. One fool docks the tail of his horse; the horse of the second gets tail caught in gate, is docked too. One notches ear of his horse; the second horse notches its ear on the fence. Finally they measure heights of their horses. The black horse is two inches taller than the white.
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
division2: J2700–J2749. The easy problem made hard.
section: J2700. The easy problem made hard.
additional_description: One fool docks the tail of his horse; the horse of the second gets tail caught in gate, is docked too. One notches ear of his horse; the second horse notches its ear on the fence. Finally they measure heights of their horses. The black horse is two inches taller than the white.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Hero immobilizes magic obstacles to his flight by foresighted bribery
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: Food theft discovered by examination of suspects’ stools
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

name: People eat tasteless berries and seaweed and regard tasty food as poisonous
chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.

chapter: J. The wise and the foolish.
